questions.question: Which of these countries had the second- highest total carrying capacity of oil pipelines operated in 2017?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We need to calculate the total carrying capacity of oil 
pipelines operated in each country by multiplying 
the carrying capacity per kilometer of the country 
by the number of kilometers of oil pipelines oper-
ated in the respective country. We can then rank the 
countries.
Calculation
We know that:
Carrying capacity per km of oil pipelines, tonnes per 
day:
= (Total carrying capacity of oil pipelines, tonnes per 
day) / (Length of oil pipelines, in km)
Hence:
Total carrying capacity of oil pipelines, tonnes per day:
= (Carrying capacity per km of oil pipelines, tonnes per 
day) * (Length of oil pipelines, in km)
Czechia: 
77.88 * (6.42 * 100)
= 77.88 * 642
≈ 50 000
Latvia:
45.56 * (4.17 * 100)
= 45.56 * 417
≈ 19 000
Norway:
286.49 * (12.88 * 100)
= 286.49 * 1 288
≈ 369 000
Romania:
121.14 * (31.12 * 100)
= 121.14 * 3 112
≈ 377 000
Therefore, Norway had the second-highest total 
carrying capacity of oil pipelines operated in 2017.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Czechia and Latvia are clearly much lower than 
Norway and Romania in both length of pipelines 
and carrying capacity per kilometer, therefore 
neither of them can be correct. You can’t tell whether 
Norway or Romania is correct without doing the 
calculations above, but you only need to calculate 
for those two countries − and if you haven’t got time 
even for that, then you have a 50/50 chance with a 
guess.

questions.question: Based on the data for 2019, and assuming that the total lengths of oil pipelines operated do not change, by how many thousand tonnes per day must Latvia increase its total carrying capacity of oil pipelines operated to match the carrying capacity per kilometer of oil pipelines operated in Czechia?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We need to calculate the total carrying capacity of 
oil pipelines operated in Latvia in 2019 and then 
determine the required total carrying capacity of oil 
pipelines operated in Latvia to match the per kilom-
eter figure of Czechia and then find the difference.
Calculation
We know that:
Total carrying capacity of oil pipelines, thousand 
tonnes per day:
= (Carrying capacity per km of oil pipelines, thousand 
tonnes per day) * (Length of oil pipelines, in km)
Total carrying capacity of oil pipelines operated (in 
thousand tonnes per day) in Latvia in 2019:
(56.05 / 1 000) * (3.39 * 100)
= 0.05605 * 339
≈ 19.00
Total carrying capacity of oil pipelines operated 
(in thousand tonnes per day) required in Latvia to 
match the per kilometer figure of Czechia:
(77.88 / 1 000) * (3.39 * 100)
= 0.07788 * 339
≈ 26.40
Increase required in the total carrying capacity of oil 
pipelines operated (in thousand tonnes per day) in 
Latvia to match the per kilometer figure of Czechia:
26.40 – 19.00
= 7.40

192
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Latvia needs to enhance the carrying capacity per 
kilometer of oil pipelines by replacing or upgrading 
the existing pipelines, not by adding to the length of 
oil pipelines. We could have multiplied the differ-
ence of the per kilometer figures of Latvia and 
Czechia with the length of oil pipelines in Latvia as 
a shortcut calculation method. Also, make sure to 
read the figures for the correct year and be careful 
with the order of magnitude of the answer required.

questions.question: If the total length of oil pipelines operated in Norway, and their carrying capacity per kilometer, continue to increase at the same rates as they did between 2015 and 2019, what is the best estimate for the total carrying capacity of oil pipelines operated ( thousand tonnes per day) in Norway in 2023
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the respective increases in the length of oil pipe-
lines and the carrying capacity per kilometer of oil 
pipelines between 2015 and 2019 to estimate the 
figures for 2023 and then use those figures to estimate 
the total carrying capacity of oil pipelines for 2023.
Calculation
Increase in length of oil pipelines operated (in kilo-
meters) in Norway between 2015 and 2019 (i.e., in 4 
years):
(15.71 – 12.45) * 100
= 3.26 *100
= 326
Increase in carrying capacity per kilometer of oil 
pipelines operated (in thousand tonnes per day) in 
Norway between 2015 and 2019 (i.e., in 4 years):
(306.81 – 296.39) / 1 000
= 10.42 / 1 000
= 0.01042
The estimated length of oil pipelines operated (in 
kilometers) in Norway in 2023:
(Length of oil pipelines in 2019) + (Estimated increase 
in length of oil pipelines from 2019 to 2023)
= (15.71 * 100) + 326
= 1 571 + 326
= 1 897
The estimated carrying capacity per kilometer of oil 
pipelines operated (in thousand tonnes per day) in 
Norway in 2023:
(Carrying capacity per km of oil pipelines in 2019) + 
(Estimated increase in carrying capacity per km of oil 
pipelines from 2019 to 2023)
= (306.81 / 1 000) + 0.01042
= 0.30681 + 0.01042
= 0.31723
We know that:
Total carrying capacity of oil pipelines, thousand 
tonnes per day:
= (Carrying capacity per km of oil pipelines, thousand 
tonnes per day) * (Length of oil pipelines, in km)
Therefore, the estimated total carrying capacity of oil 
pipelines operated (in thousand tonnes per day) in 
Norway for 2023 is: 
0.31723 * 1 897
≈ 602
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You may have spotted that the answer options are 
very spread apart. This is a clue that estimating 
could be possible. If you round down the figures for 
2019 for Norway, the carrying capacity per kilom-
eter is about 300 tonnes per day and there are 1500 
kilometers of pipelines. Multiply those together 
and you get 450,000 tonnes per day. A quick look 
at the figures shows that while Norway’s carrying 
capacity per kilometer isn’t increasing much, the 
total length of pipelines is increasing by around a 
quarter in 4 years. This means we are looking for 
an answer which adds something like 25% to the 
450,000, and a bit more on top to compensate for the 
rounding down. The only possible correct answer 
option is D. 
Of course, you still have to be careful with the order 
of magnitude and notice that the figures in the 
answer options are in thousand tonnes per day. If you 
miss this, you will mistakenly choose answer option 
E.

questions.question: What was the change in the difference (in millions) between the populations of Poland and the United Kingdom from 2015 to 2018?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We need first to estimate the populations of Poland 
and the United Kingdom in 2015 and 2018. We can 
then work out the difference in population between 
the two countries for those years, and find the 
change in the difference.

193
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Calculation
We know that:
Settlement area per capita (in sq km per capita):
= Total settlement area (in sq km)/ total population
Hence:
Total population:
= Total settlement area (in sq km) / Settlement area per 
capita (in sq km per capita)
Total population (in millions) of Poland in 2015:
23 699 / 623.9 ≈ 38.0
Total population (in millions) of United Kingdom in 
2015:
28 035 / 430.5 ≈ 65.1
Difference (in millions) in the total populations of 
Poland and United Kingdom in 2015:
65.1 – 38.0 = 27.1
Total population (in millions) of Poland in 2018:
24 068 / 633.7 ≈ 38.0
Total population (in millions) of United Kingdom in 
2018:
28 294 / 426.9 ≈ 66.3
Difference (in millions) in the total populations of 
Poland and United Kingdom in 2018:
66.3 – 38.0 = 28.3
The change in the difference (in millions) of the total 
populations of Poland and United Kingdom from 
2015 to 2018:
28.3 – 27.1 = 1.2
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Note that the difference between the two popula-
tions has increased. Therefore, the change in the 
difference must be positive, meaning either answer 
D or E. 
Instead of first converting the settlement area from 
square meters per capita to square kilometers per 
capita by multiplying with a factor of 1 000 000 
and then converting the total populations back to 
millions by dividing by 1 000 000, we can simply use 
the figures as they are to save time. The results will 
be correct.

questions.question: In 2018, the total land area of Belgium was 30 650 square kilometers while its total settlement area as a percentage of its total land area was 3 times as much as that of Poland in 2012. What was the approximate total land area (in square kilometers) of Poland in 2012?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the total settled area of 
Belgium in 2018 as a percentage of its total land area 
to determine a similar percentage for Poland in 2012 
based on the given relationship. We can then use 
this percentage to calculate the total land area of 
Poland in 2012.
Calculation
We know that:
Total settlement area as % of the total land area:
= (Total settlement area /Total land area) *100% 
Approximate total settlement area as a percentage of 
the total land area in Belgium in 2018:
(6 650 / 30 650) * (100%)
≈ 0.217 * 100%
= 21.7%
In 2018, Belgium’s total settlement area as a 
percentage of its total land area was 3 times as much 
as that of Poland in 2012. Therefore, Poland’s total 
settlement area as a percentage of its total land area 
in 2012 was:
= 21.7% / 3
≈ 7.2%
Hence, Poland’s total land area (in square kilom-
eters) in 2012 was:
= Total settlement area in 2012/Total settlement area in 
2012 as % of total land area
= 22 606 / 7.2%
= 22 606 / (7.2 / 100)
= 22 606 / 0.072
≈ 314 000
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The answer options have significantly varying 
values. Therefore, approximate calculations could 
have saved time and still given us the correct 
answer. Option A is less than, and option B the same 
as, the settlement area of Poland in 2012, so neither 
can possibly be the total land area. Option C is three 
times the total land area of Belgium. We already 
know that the percentage of Belgium’s settlement 
was three times the percentage of Poland and not 
the total land area. Hence, it is also incorrect. Option 
D would result if the percentage of the settlement 

194
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
area in Belgium was equal to that of Poland, which 
is not the case. Option E is therefore correct.
Note that the given total land area and settlement 
areas are both in square kilometers, hence no unit 
conversions are required.

questions.question: Based on the change in settlement area per capita and total settlement area of Belgium from 2012 to 2015, and assuming a linear rate of growth in population, what is the best estimate of its total population (in millions) in 2014?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the total populations of Belgium 
in 2012 and 2015 to estimate the total population of 
Belgium in 2014.
Calculation
We know that:
Total population: 
= Total settlement area (in sq km)/ Settlement area per 
capita (in sq km per capita)
Hence:
Total population in millions:
= Total settlement area (in sq km)/ Settlement area per 
capita (in sq meters per capita)
Total population (in millions) of Belgium in 2012:
5 973 / 537.8
≈ 11.11
Total population (in millions) of Belgium in 2015:
6 557 / 581.6
≈ 11.27
Total change in Belgium’s total population (in 
millions) from 2012 to 2015:
11.27 – 11.11
= 0.16
Assuming linear rate of growth, annual change in 
Belgium’s total population (in millions) from 2012 to 
2015:
= 0.16 / 3
≈ 0.0533
Estimated change in Belgium’s total population (in 
millions) from 2012 to 2014:
0.0533 * 2
≈ 0.11
The best estimate of Belgium’s total population (in 
millions) in 2014:
(Belgium’s total population in 2012) 
+ (Estimated change in Belgium’s total population (in 
millions) from 2012 to 2014)
= 11.11 + 0.11
= 11.22
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is important to account for the difference in units 
when finding the exact population. 
Here we have four very similar and therefore plau-
sible options to choose from: B, C, D and E. However, 
our very first calculation tells us that B is the popu-
lation in 2012, so that will not be right. Our second 
calculation shows that E is the population in 2015, so 
that will not be right. This leaves only options C and 
D. You may have noticed the approximately equal 
gaps between the values in options B, C, D and E. 
This probably means that option C is the estimate for 
2013 and option D is the likely estimate for 2014.

questions.question: What was the difference in the number of national air transport passengers in Dec-202 between Germany and Austria?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must estimate the number of national air 
transport passengers in Germany and Austria in 
Dec-2022 by multiplying the total number of passen-
gers by the relevant percentages. Then we can find 
the difference between the two.
Calculation
We know that:
Number of national air transport passengers:
= Total passengers * Percentage of national air 
transport passengers
As we can see, a bar graph has been provided 
instead of exact figures for air transport passengers 
by country. It is an indication that we will have to 
employ estimation and mental arithmetic. We can 
read an approximate number for total air transport 
passengers in Dec-2022 from the bar graph. Austria 
had approximately 2 million and Germany had 
around 11 million.
Similarly, we can round off the percentages of 
national air transport passengers in Dec-2022. 
Austria’s percentage was almost 1% and Germany’s 
was around 7%.
National air transport passengers (in millions) in 
Germany in Dec-2022:

195
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
11 * 7%
= (10 + 1) * 7% = 10 * 7% + 1 * 7%
= 0.7 + 0.07 ≈ 0.77
National air transport passengers (in millions) in 
Austria in Dec-2022:
2 * 1% ≈ 0.02
The difference in the number of national air trans-
port passengers in Dec-2022 between Germany and 
Austria:
(0.77 – 0.02) * 1 000 000
= 0.75 * 1 000 000
≈ 750 000
Option C is the closest in value. Hence, it must be 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must be careful to read the data for the relevant 
period. If the figures for an incorrect period are used, 
the result may match one of the incorrect options. 
Although our result was off by around 5 500 passen-
gers, the correct result was comfortably close to our 
approximation. The net result was that we saved 
time and still got the correct answer.

questions.question: What was the change in the number of all international air transport passengers in Spain from Feb-2023 to Apr-2023?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the combined interna-
tional air transport passenger percentages in Spain 
for Feb-2023 and Apr-2023 and then use them to 
calculate the number of international air transport 
passengers in both periods. We can then find the 
difference to determine the change.
Calculation
We know that:
Number of national air transport passengers:
= Total passengers * Percentage of national air 
transport passengers
The combined international air transport passenger 
percentages in Spain for Feb-2023:
45.8% + 32.1% = 77.9% ≈ 80%
The combined international air transport passenger 
percentages in Spain for Apr-2023:
48.0% + 32.5% = 80.5% ≈ 80%
As we can see, the combined percentage of interna-
tional air transport passengers has changed mini-
mally and can be assumed to have remained constant 
at approximately 80%. Therefore, the change in the 
number of all international air transport passengers 
is simply 80% of the change in the number of all 
air transport passengers in Spain from Feb-2023 to 
Apr-2023.
Total number of air transport passengers in Spain 
in Feb-2023 was approximately 13 million and in 
Apr-2023 it was around 20 million.
Approximate change (in millions) in the number of 
all international air transport passengers in Spain 
from Feb-2023 to Apr-2023:
= 80% * (20 - 13)
= (80 / 100) * 7 ≈ 5.5
Option E is the closest in value. Hence, it must be 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It was an efficient shortcut to assume a constant 
rounded percentage of international air transport 
passengers for both periods. It simplified our calcu-
lations significantly.
It is vital to read the values for the correct periods. 
Had figures for Dec-2022 been read instead of 
Feb-2023, the result would have matched with 
another option. You must also note that the number 
of total and international air transport passengers 
in Apr-2023 was greater than the those in Feb-2023, 
hence the change must be an increase, eliminating 
answer options A and B.

questions.question: Based on the change in settlement area per capita and total settlement area of Belgium from 2012 to 2015, and assuming a linear rate of growth in population, what is the best estimate of its total population (in millions) in 2014?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must estimate a percentage for international 
(Extra EU-27) air transport passengers in Germany 
in Dec-2023 as well as the total number of air trans-
port passengers.
Calculation
Change in percentage of international (Extra EU-27) 
air transport passengers in Germany in 2 months 
from Feb-2023 to Apr-2023:
43.0% – 45.4% = –2.4%

196
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Therefore, assuming a linear model, the change in 
the percentage in the next 8 months will be four 
times that of the previous 2 months:
4 * –2.4% = –9.6%
Hence, the approximate percentage of international 
(Extra EU-27) air transport passengers in Germany 
in Dec-2023 will be:
43.0% – 9.6% = 33.4% ≈ 35%
From the bar graph, the total number of air transport 
passengers in Germany in Feb-2023 was approxi-
mately 10 million and in Apr-2023 it was around 15 
million.
Change in the total number (in millions) of air 
transport passengers in Germany in 2 months from 
Feb-2023 to Apr-2023:
15 – 10 = 5
Therefore, assuming a linear model, the change in 
the total in the next 8 months will be four times that 
of the previous 2 months:
4 * 5 = 20
Hence, the approximate total number (in millions) 
of air transport passengers in Germany in Dec-2023 
will be:
15 + 20 = 35
The anticipated number (in millions) of international 
(Extra EU-27) air transport passengers in Germany 
in Dec-2023:
35 * 35% = 35 * (35 / 100) = 12.25
Option D is the closest in value. Hence, it must be 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Despite the falling percentage of international (Extra 
EU-27) air transport passengers in Germany, the 
significant increase in the total number of air trans-
port passengers in Germany will cause the number 
of that type of passengers to increase. Therefore, do 
not mark option A as correct in haste.
An alternative method of calculation would have been 
to calculate the number of international (Extra EU-27) 
air transport passengers in Feb-2023 and in Apr-2023 
and then base the prediction on those two figures.
The projected total passenger numbers for Dec-2023 
(35 million), when compared with the same month in 
2022 (11 million), will probably seem very unlikely in 
real-world terms. However, you must always work 
only with the data provided and the assumptions 
given in the question text and not import any other 
knowledge or reasoning.

questions.question: What was the difference in the average spee (in m/s) of the athletes in the 1st and 10th fin positions, in the last 5 km of the race walk?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the difference between the 15 
km and the 20 km times and then use the speed, 
distance and time formula to calculate the average 
speeds of each athlete. Then we can find the differ-
ence in average speeds.
Calculation
Time taken (in seconds) by Maria Perez (athlete in 
1st place) for the last 5 km:
1:26:51 – 1:06:04
= 26:51 – 06:04 = (26 * 60 + 51) – (6 * 60 + 4)
= 1 611 – 364 = 1 247
Time taken (in seconds) by Olena Sobchuk (athlete in 
10th place) for the last 5 km:
1:28:50 – 1:06:17
= 28:50 – 06:17 = (28 * 60 + 50) – (6 * 60 + 17)
= 1 730 – 377 = 1 353
Speed = Distance / Time
Average speed (in m/s) of Maria Perez in the last 5 
km:
(5 * 1 000) / 1 247 ≈ 4.00
Average speed (in m/s) of Olena Sobchuk in the last 
5 km:
(5 * 1 000) / 1 353 ≈ 3.70
The difference in the average speeds (in m/s) of 
athletes in 1st and 10th positions in the last 5 km of 
the race walk:
4.00 – 3.70 = 0.30
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The units required are m/s. Therefore, the numbers 
must be converted from km to m and from hours, 
minutes, seconds to seconds. You must also first 
determine the average speeds of each athlete and 
then subtract them. You cannot divide the distance 
by the difference in times to find the difference in 
average speeds.

197
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
As you may have noticed, we ignored 1 hour from 
each of the 15 km and 20 km times when subtracting. 
This saves valuable time in the test.
If you have no time to do proper calculations and 
need to make a guess, just think about the answer 
options. A, B and C are obviously of a different 
order of magnitude to D and E, so which group will 
include the correct answer? E is a round number so 
you can do a quick bit of mental arithmetic. A differ-
ence of 4 metres per second is 240 metres per minute, 
or nearly 1 km every 4 minutes. However, we know 
that the difference in the time the two athletes took 
to cover 20 km was only 2 minutes. D and E cannot 
possibly be right.

questions.question: Assuming that athletes ran at constant spee between the noted times, how far behind Antonella Palmisano was Zhenxia Ma (in meters) when Ma was at the 19 km mark?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the time difference for 
Palmisano and Ma to reach the 19 km mark. Then 
we can work out the distance covered by Palmisano 
in that amount of time.
Calculation
Difference between times (in seconds) when 
Palmisano and Ma reached the 19 km mark:
1:24:02 – 1:23:16
= 24:02 – 23:16 = (24 * 60 + 2) – (23 * 60 + 16)
= 1 442 – 1 396 = 46
Time required (in seconds) by Palmisano to cover the 
final 1 km:
1:27:26 – 1:23:16
= 27:26 – 23:16 = (27 * 60 + 26) – (23 * 60 + 16)
= 1 646 – 1 396 = 250
Average speed (in m/s) of Palmisano for the final 1 
km:
1 000 / 250 = 4
We know that: 
Distance = Speed * Time
Distance covered by Palmisano (in meters) in 46 
seconds after passing the 19 km mark (i.e., the 
distance between Antonella Palmisano and Zhenxia 
Ma (in meters) when Ma was at the 19 km mark):
4 * 46 = 184
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Be careful to read the data for the relevant times. 
Remember also that the distance between the 
athletes is required when Ma is at the 19 km mark, 
not when Palmisano is at that point.
Be careful not to use Ma’s average speed to calculate 
the distance. It is convenient to carry out calcula-
tions in meters and seconds. Again, we can ignore 
the complete hour from time difference calculations.
In case you need to make an educated guess, you 
can do a bit of mental arithmetic. The difference in 
times for the two athletes to reach the 19 km mark 
was around 45 seconds. The athletes, in general, took 
around an hour and a half to cover 20 km. That is 
around 10 km in 45 minutes and around (10/60) km 
in 45 seconds. Hence, the distance between the two 
athletes will be around 165 m. Options C and D are 
good guesses.

questions.question: If the times of the other athletes stayed the same, by how many seconds would Ana Cabecinha have missed a shared 1st place had she covered the 20 km distance 2% faster than she did?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate Cabecinha’s average speed for 
the 20 km race walk and then find the average speed 
which is 2% faster. Then, use the faster speed to 
calculate the time it would take her to cover 20 km. 
Finally, find the difference in time with the athlete 
in 1st place.
Calculation
Cabecinha’s average speed (in m/s) in the 20 km race 
walk:
(20 * 1 000) / (1:28:49)
= 20 000 / ((60 + 28) * 60 + 49)
= 20 000 / 5 329 ≈ 3.753
Average speed (in m/s) that is 2% faster than her 
actual speed:
3.753 * (100% + 2%)
= 3.753 * 1.02 ≈ 3.83
Time = Distance / Speed
Time required (in seconds) by Cabecinha to complete 
20 km race walk at a 2% faster speed:
(20 * 1 000) / 3.83
≈ 5 222

198
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Time taken (in seconds) by Maria Perez (1st place) to 
complete the 20 km race walk:
1:26:51
= ((60 + 26) * 60 + 51)
= 5 211
Number of seconds by which Ana Cabecinha would 
have missed a shared 1st position had she covered 
the 20 km distance 2% faster:
5 222 – 5 211 = 11
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is important to convert the complete times 
(including the hours) to seconds for calculation since 
these are not time difference calculations. Be careful 
not to increase Cabecinha’s race walk time by 2%. 
That would actually make her slower.
Cabecinha’s time was around an hour and a half 
to complete the race walk and the time difference 
between her and Perez was almost 120 seconds. To 
increase her speed by 2%, she must cut her total 
time by around 2% as well. 2% of 90 minutes is 1.8 
minutes ≈ 110 seconds. If we subtract 110 seconds 
from the existing time difference of the two athletes, 
we get 120 – 110=10 seconds. Option C would be the 
best guess.

questions.question: What was the number of monthly active X (formerly Twitter) users as a percentage of monthly active Facebook users in the first quarter of 2016 (Q1 ’16)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must estimate the number of monthly active 
users of X as well as Facebook in Q1 ’16 from the 
graphs and then calculate the former as a percentage 
of the latter.
Calculation
As we can see, graphs have been provided instead of 
exact figures for monthly active users of two social 
media platforms. It is an indication that we will have 
to use estimation and mental arithmetic. 
We can read approximate numbers for monthly 
active users of X and Facebook in Q1 ’16 from the 
respective graphs. X had approximately 310 million 
monthly active users. Facebook had approximately 
1 600 million monthly active users in Q4 ’15 and 
approximately 1 700 million in Q2 ’16. The inter-
mediate point (Q1 ’16) between these two points is 
around 1 650 million.
We know that five times 310 is 1 550. Alternatively, 
310 is one-fifth, or 20%, of 1 550. Hence, 310 as a 
percentage of 1 650 should be less than 20%. Option 
A is the obvious choice.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The graphs provide data with a different time range. 
You must be careful to read the data for the relevant 
period only.
It is critical to note, from the question, which number 
is required as a percentage of the other. If the values 
are swapped by mistake, you will end up choosing 
an incorrect option, having a percentage higher than 
500%, as correct.
As you can see, we did not calculate the exact 
percentage required in the question, but only esti-
mated a range for the target value to save precious 
time.

questions.question: What was the number of male Facebook use in the age range 25-44 in January 2023?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the number of Facebook 
monthly active users in January 2023, which falls 
in Q1 ’23. Then, we must add up the percentages of 
male users in the age ranges 25-34 and 35-44. Finally, 
we can calculate the number of male Facebook users 
in the age range 25-44 in January 2023.
Calculation
Facebook had approximately 2 970 million monthly 
active users in Q4 ’22 and approximately 3 030 
million in Q2 ’23. The intermediate point (Q1 ’23) 
between these two points is around 3 000 million.
From the age and gender distribution graph, we can 
see that there were 17.6% male Facebook users in the 
age range 25-34 and 10.9% in the age range 35-44. 
Together, there were 17.6% + 10.9% = 28.5% (approx-
imately 30%) monthly active male Facebook users in 
the age range 25-44 in January 2023.
Approximate number (in millions) of male Facebook 
users in the age range 25-44 in January 2023:
= 3 000 * 30%
= 3 000 * 0.30
= 900
Option D is the closest in value. Hence, it must be 
correct.

199
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It was efficient to assume a rounded percentage 
of male Facebook users in the given age range. It 
simplified our calculation significantly.
It is vital to read the values for the correct period. 
Similarly, to read the percentages for the correct age 
groups and gender from the distribution graph. In 
case of an error, the incorrect options might match 
your answer. It is easy to confuse the age range 25-44 
with 25-34. Therefore, care must be taken in reading 
the correct percentages.

questions.question: Based on the monthly active users’ growth rate trend from Q1 ’13 to Q1 ’15, what was the difference (in millions) between the projected and the actual number of monthly active users of X (formerly Twitter) in Q1 ‘17?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must estimate the number of monthly active 
users of X in Q1 ’13 and Q1 ’15 and find the change 
between them. We must use this difference to calcu-
late the projected number of monthly active users 
in Q1 ’17. The difference between the projected and 
actual number of monthly active users in Q1 ’17 will 
be our solution.
Calculation
The approximate number of monthly active users of 
X in Q1 ’13 was 200 million and in Q1 ’15 was around 
300 million. Therefore, the increase in two years was 
300 – 200 = 100 million.
The projected monthly active users of X in Q1 ‘17, 
based on the trend from Q1 ’13 to Q1 15, was:
(Number of monthly active users in Q1 ’15) + (Change 
in two years in monthly active users from Q1 ’13 to Q1 
’15)
= 300 million + 100 million
= 400 million
From the graph, the approximate number of the 
actual monthly active users of X in Q1 ’17 was 325 
million.
The difference (in millions) between the projected 
and the actual number of monthly active users of X 
(formerly Twitter) in Q1 ’17 was:
400 – 325 = 75
Option B is the closest in value. Hence, it must be 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Although we could have calculated the annual 
change (increase) in the number of monthly active 
users of X to calculate the projected number for Q1 
’17, it was quicker and convenient to just add the 
figure for two years directly to the number in Q1 ‘15.
Make sure that you read the data for X and not 
Facebook.
As can be seen from the graph of X, the number of 
monthly active users was increasing almost linearly 
up until Q1 ’15 after which the number almost stag-
nated between 300 million and 350 million. Hence, 
all projections based on the data before Q1 ’15 would 
have proven incorrect.

questions.question: If ‘ICT, media, computers, business and financial services’ accounted for approximately 49% of all services imported by the European Union in 2014, what was th total value of services imported (in millions of euros) that year?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the given percentage of the noted category to 
construct an equation for the sum of all services 
imported in 2014. This will allow us to calculate the 
total value of services imported by the European 
Union in 2014.
Calculation
Let X be the total value of services imported (in 
millions of euros) by the European Union in 2014.
Therefore, the value of services imported in the 
‘ICT…’ category in 2014 must be:
= 49% * X 
= 0.49X
The sum of all services imported in 2014 was:
X = 19 429 + 15 384 + 27 282 + 3 901 + 158 618 + 0.49X 
+ 87 421 + 1 778
X = 313 813 + 0.49X
X – 0.49X = 313 813
0.51X = 313 813
X = 313 813 / 0.51
X ≈ 615 320
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The question refers to the year 2014 only. Therefore, 
you must take care only to use data from that year. 
Incorrect options for 49% of 2016’s total and 2016’s 
total itself have been provided. The sum of all 
amounts other than the mentioned category has also 
been provided as an incorrect option.

200
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Since the percentage of the category is 49% (approx-
imately 50%), the sum of all other services almost 
equals that of ‘ICT…’ alone. Hence, we can simply 
double the sum of all other services imported to 
make a guess.

questions.question: If the services imported related to ‘Construction, wood, glass, stone, basic metals, housing, electrical appliances, he furniture’ and ‘Government, military and other’ stayed the same as a percentage of the respective total services imported in 2014 and 2018, what was the value of services imported in the ‘Government, military and other’ category in 2018 (in millions of euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must construct an equation such that the factor 
of increase in the ‘Construction…’ category from 
2014 to 2018 is applied to the ‘Government…’ cate-
gory since the percentages of both these categories 
have remained constant.
Calculation
Let G be the value of services imported in the 
‘Government…’ category in 2018 (in millions of 
euros).
Hence:
G = (Services imported in the Construction category in 
2018 / Services imported in the Construction category 
in 2014) * Government services imported in 2014 
G = (37 174 / 27 282) * 1 778
G ≈ 1.363 * 1 778
G = 2 423
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Each of the two mentioned categories represented 
the same respective fraction of the total services 
imported in 2014 and 2018. Hence, they both 
increased by the same factor between those years 
along with the totals. However, it is unnecessary to 
calculate the exact percentage of the total that each 
category represents.
A quick glance at the import figures of the 
‘Construction…’ category tells us that the increase 
from 2014 to 2016 was approximately 5 000 and was 
almost the same from 2016 to 2018. We should expect 
a similar trend from the ‘Government…’ category. 
The increase from 2014 to 2016 was around 300. 
Another increase of 300 should bring the 2018 figure 
into the vicinity of 2 400. Hence, option C would be a 
good guess to make.

questions.question: What additional value (in millions of euros) of ‘Health, pharmaceuticals, education, cultural, sport’ services should have been imported by the European Union in 2018, as compared to 2016, to match the percentage increase of ‘Transport equipment and services, travel, postal services’ imported from 2016 to 2018?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must make an equation to apply the factor 
increase of the ‘Transport…’ category from 2016 
to 2018, to the ‘Health…’ category in order to find 
the additional value of imports required in the 
‘Health…’ category.
Calculation
Let H, be the additional value of services imported 
(in millions of euros) in the ‘Health…’ category 
in 2018 to match the percentage increase in the 
‘Transport…’ category imported from 2016 to 2018.
Therefore:
H = (((Services imported in the Transport category in 
2018) / (Services imported in the Transport category in 
2016)) * (Health services imported in 2016)) – (Health 
services imported in 2016)
H = (189 749 / 165 437) * 145 177 – 145 177
H ≈ 1.147 * 145 177 – 145 177
H ≈ 166 518 – 145 177 
H = 21 341
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The additional value of imports in 2018 in ‘Health…’ 
services is required and not the revised value of 
imports in the ‘Health…’ services in 2018.
It is very important to use the amounts of 2016 and 
2018 for the noted categories only. If incorrect figures 
are used, you might choose one of the incorrect 
options.
The increase in the imports of ‘Transport…’ was 
around 24 000 from 2016 to 2018. Since the 2016 
imports of ‘Health…’ were about 12% lower, a corre-
sponding increase of 12% lower is required. (100 – 
12%) = 88% of 24 000 is around 21 000. Hence, option 
C would be the best guess.

questions.question: In 2017, what would have been the approximate price (in euros) of a particular product in Ireland that had a price of €50 in Hungary?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the comparative price levels of Hungary and 
Ireland in 2017 to calculate the equivalent price in 
Ireland.

201
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Calculation
The comparative price level in Hungary in 2017 was 
65.9. This means that if something had an average 
price of €100.0 in the EU, it would be priced at €65.9 
in Hungary. Similarly, the comparative price level in 
Ireland in 2017 was 131.6. This means that if some-
thing had an average price of Euros €100.0 in the EU, 
it would be priced at €131.6 in Ireland.
Therefore, the EU average price (in Euros) of the €50 
product in Hungary would be:
(EU average price level / Hungary comparative price 
level) * (Product price in Hungary)
= (100 / 65.9) * 50
≈ 75.87
The price (in euros) of the same product in Ireland 
would be:
= (Ireland comparative price level / EU average price 
level) * (EU average product price)
(131.6 /100) * 75.87
≈ 99.85
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The shortcut method of calculating the equivalent 
price would be:
(Ireland comparative price level / Hungary comparative 
price level) * (Product price in Hungary)
= (131.6 / 65.9) * 50 ≈ 99.85
Consider that Ireland was almost twice as expen-
sive (65 * 2 = 130) as Hungary in 2017. Hence the €50 
product would cost almost twice as much in Ireland, 
i.e., around €100. Options B and C would be the best 
guesses.

questions.question: What was the difference between the percentage change in price levels in Japan as compared to those in Germany from 2012 to 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the comparative price levels of Germany and 
Japan in 2012 and 2022 to determine the difference 
between the percentage change in the price levels in 
Japan as compared to those in Germany from 2012 
to 2022.
Calculation
The comparative price level in Germany in 2012 was 
104.9 and in 2022 was 108.9. Similarly, the compara-
tive price level in Japan in 2012 was 138.8 and in 2022 
was 112.1. This means that price levels in Germany 
increased over 10 years and those in Japan decreased 
over the same period.
Percentage change in comparative price level in 
Germany from 2012 to 2022:
= ((Comparative price level in 2022 – Comparative price 
level in 2012) / (Comparative price level in 2012)) * 100%
= ((108.9 – 104.9) / 104.9) * 100%
= (4.0 / 104.9) * 100%
≈ 0.0381 / 100% = + 3.81%
Percentage change in comparative price level in 
Japan from 2012 to 2022:
= ((Comparative price level in 2022 – Comparative price 
level in 2012) / (Comparative price level in 2012)) * 100%
= ((112.1 – 138.8) / 138.8) * 100%
= (– 26.7 / 138.8) * 100%
≈ – 0.1924 / 100% = –19.24%
The difference between the percentage changes in 
the price levels in Japan as compared to those in 
Germany from 2012 to 2022:
= |(Percentage change in Japan) – (Percentage change 
in Germany)|
= – 19.24% – (+3.81%)
= – 19.24% – 3.81% = –23.05%
= 23.05%
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
There are quite a few calculations here, with plenty 
of potential for going wrong, so can we get to an 
answer which is close enough without doing full 
calculations? Think about it this way. Germany’s 
price level increased by 4 over ten years, which is 
approximately 4%. Japan’s price level, on the other 
hand, decreased by around 26 over ten years, which 
is approximately 20%. Hence the difference is 4% – 
(–20) = 24%. Therefore, option E should be correct 
because it is the closest value to our estimate.

questions.question: What should have been the comparative price level in Poland in 2017, if it were to match the percentage of increase in price level in the United States from 2012 to 2017?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Compute the percentage of increase in price level in 
the United States from 2012 to 2017 and then find 
the comparative price level for Poland in 2017 that 
would have matched the percentage in Poland.

202
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Calculation
The comparative price level in the United States in 
2012 was 98.3 and in 2017 was 118.0. Similarly, the 
comparative price level in Poland in 2012 was 57.6.
The percentage of increase in price level in the United 
States from 2012 to 2017:
= ((Comparative price level in 2017 – Comparative price 
level in 2012) / (Comparative price level in 2012)) * 100%
= ((118.0 – 98.3) / 98.3) * 100%
= (19.7 / 98.3) * 100%
≈ 0.2004 / 100% = 20.04%
Let P, be the comparative price level in Poland in 
2017 to match the percentage of increase in price 
level of the United States from 2012 to 2017.
To match the percentage of increase of price level in 
the United States from 2012 to 2017, the comparative 
price level in Poland in 2017 should have been:
Target percentage of increase in price level:
= ((Comparative price level in 2017 – Comparative price 
level in 2012) / (Comparative price level in 2012)) * 100%
20.04% = ((P – 57.6) / 57.6) * 100%
0.2004 = (P – 57.6) / 57.6
P – 57.6 = 0.2004 * 57.6 ≈ 11.5
P = 11.5 + 57.6 = 69.1
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The actual comparative price level in Poland in 2017, 
i.e. 58.8, is irrelevant to the calculation. We do not 
need the actual comparative price level, but one that 
would equate the percentage increase in price level 
of the United States in the specified 5 years with that 
of Poland. It is also crucial to distinguish between 
the percentage increase in comparative price levels as 
opposed to the simple increase in comparative price 
levels.
Once again, we have lots of calculations that can be 
greatly simplified. The comparative price level in 
the United States increased by around 20, which is 
almost equal to 20%. A 20% increase in 57.6, i.e., the 
2012 comparative price level of Poland, would give 
you 57.6 *1.2 = 69.12. Option B is the closest value.

questions.question: What was the change in the number of cases per hospital bed from 1997 to 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must estimate the number of cases per hospital 
bed for 1997 as well as for 2021 and then find the 
difference between the two.
Calculation
We know that:
Number of cases per hospital bed: 
= Total number of cases / Total number of hospital 
beds 
The total number of cases in Germany in 1997 was 
about 16 500 thousand and in 2021 approximately 
16 750 thousand. The total number of hospital beds 
in Germany in 1997 was about 600 thousand and in 
2021 approximately 500 thousand. 
The approximate number of cases per hospital bed in 
Germany in 1997:
= 16 500 / 600
≈ 27.5
The approximate number of cases per hospital bed in 
Germany in 2021:
= 16 750 / 500
≈ 33.5
The approximate change in the number of cases per 
hospital bed from 1997 to 2021:
= 33.5 – 27.5
= +6
Option D is the closest value to our estimate and is 
therefore the correct option to choose.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
As always when there is a table with lots of rows 
and columns you have to be careful to get your data 
from the right ones. Here, for example, it is easy to 
confuse 1997 with 1991, since 1991 is the first row in 
the table. As well as calculating the ratio correctly, 
you must subtract the ratio for 1997 from the ratio for 
2021 and not the other way around.
The data provided in the table is accurate, but the 
numbers are also quite large, making it easier to approx-
imate these figures to get an estimated ratio rather than 
do full calculations. As can be seen, our result from 
approximating was quite close to the correct option 
and hence we were able to save valuable time.

203
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: What was the average number of unoccupie beds per hospital in Germany in 2015?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the bed occupancy rate and the total number of 
beds to find the average number of beds occupied 
in 2015. Subtract this from the number of beds to 
get the average number of unoccupied beds. Divide 
this result by the number of hospitals to get the 
average number of unoccupied beds per hospital in 
Germany in 2015.
Calculation
We know that:
Bed occupancy rate:
= Average number of beds occupied / Total number of 
beds
Hence:
Average number of beds occupied:
= Bed occupancy rate * Total number of beds
The approximate number of beds in 2015 was 500 
000 and the approximate number of hospitals in 2015 
was 2 000.
Average number of beds occupied in 2015:
≈ 77.5% * 500 000
= 387 500
Average number unoccupied beds in 2015:
= Total beds – Average number of beds occupied
≈ 500 000 – 387 500 = 112 500
Average number unoccupied beds per hospital in 
2015:
≈ 112 500 / 2 000 ≈ 56
Option B is the closest in value. Hence, it is probably 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
In complicated tables it is easy to get distracted by 
irrelevant data. The data for the number of cases, 
occupancy days and length of stay is irrelevant to 
this question. Also, you must remember that the 
question asks about the number of unoccupied beds 
as opposed to the number of occupied beds.

questions.question: In which of the following situations will th bed occupancy rate be closest to 60%? d e

options.text: Total beds = 501 200, Total occupancy days = 105 million
Total beds = 403 700, Total occupancy days = 105 million
Total beds = 798 800, Total occupancy days = 140 million
Total beds = 252 100, Total occupancy days = 70 million
Total beds = 249 500, Total occupancy days = 35 million

questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first find the relationship between the 
bed occupancy rate, total beds and total occupancy 
days. Then, we can substitute data given in each of 
the options to find the bed occupancy rate closest 
to 60%.
Calculation
There is a certain total number of beds. Each bed 
is available for 365 days (approximately) per year. 
Hence, these beds are available for the following 
number of days in a year:
Bed occupancy available in a year = Total beds * 365
The bed occupancy rate can then be found by:
((Total occupancy days) / (Bed occupancy available in a 
year)) * 100%
= ((Total occupancy days) / (Total beds * 365)) * 100*
We must now calculate the bed occupancy rate for 
each option based on approximate values. Let us 
assume 350 days per year for easier and quicker 
calculations. 
Option A:
≈ ((105 * 1 000 000) / (500 000 * 350)) * 100% = 60%
Option B:
≈ ((105 * 1 000 000) / (400 000 * 350)) * 100% = 75%
Option C:
≈ ((105 * 1 000 000) / (800 000 * 350)) * 100% = 50%
Option D:
≈ ((105 * 1 000 000) / (250 000 * 350)) * 100% = 80%
Option E:
≈ ((105 * 1 000 000) / (250 000 * 350)) * 100% = 40%
Based on the approximate calculations, option A is 
the closest in value. Hence, it is probably correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
We do not actually require any data from the table 
for this question. Also bear in mind that we got 
the correct answer with the very first calculation, 
starting with Option A. In the context of an exam 
there is no point carrying on with all the calcula-
tions for options B, C, D and E as well. 

204
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: What was Austria’s inland consumption of natural gas as a percentage of its electricity available for final consumption in 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must convert one of the consumption levels 
provided into the units of the other by using the 
conversion factor and then we can calculate the 
required percentage.
Calculation
In 2021, Austria’s inland consumption of natural gas 
was 360.2 million Gigajoules and Austria’s electricity 
available for final consumption was 66 861 GWh.
Austria’s inland consumption of natural gas in 2021 
(in GWh):
= (Austria’s inland consumption of natural gas in 2021 
in millions of gigajoules * 1 000 000) / (Gigajoules per 
GWh)
= (360.2 * 1 000 000) / 3 600
≈ (3.6 * 100 * 1 000 000) / (3.6 * 1 000)
= 100 000
Austria’s inland consumption of natural gas as 
a percentage of its electricity available for final 
consumption in 2021:
= ((Austria’s inland consumption of natural gas in 
2021 in GWh) / (Austria’s electricity available for final 
consumption in 2021 in GWh)) * 100%
= (100 000 / 66 861) * 100%
≈ (100 000 / (100 000 * 2/3)) * 100%
= 3/2* 100%
= 150%
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is a must to read the data for the correct year and 
country. The bar graphs are oriented differently, 
which may cause problems with correctly reading 
the data, so be careful. 
It is important to note that there are two conversions 
to be accounted for in the calculation. One is the 
change in the order of magnitude from millions of 
Gigajoules to Gigajoules and the other is the conver-
sion of units from Gigajoules to GWh. Approximating 
the result of the first conversion made it significantly 
easier for us to estimate the percentage solution. 
Also, be careful not to calculate the reciprocal of 
what is required because it is provided as the incor-
rect option C.

questions.question: By how much more did Slovakia’s inland consumption of natural gas decline (in GWh) as compared to the electricity available for final consumption from 2021 to 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Calculate the reductions in Slovakia’s inland 
consumption of natural gas and the electricity avail-
able for final consumption from 2021 to 2022. Then 
convert them to common units (GWh) and order of 
magnitude to calculate the solution.
Calculation
The decline in Slovakia’s inland consumption 
of natural gas from 2021 to 2022 (in millions of 
Gigajoules):
= 177.2 – 211.7
= – 34.5
The decline in Slovakia’s inland consumption of 
natural gas from 2021 to 2022 (in GWh):
= (– 34.5 * 1 000 000) / 3 600
≈ – 9 500
The decline in Slovakia’s electricity available for 
final consumption from 2021 to 2022 (in GWh):
= 22 451 – 26 457 
≈ – 4 000
The additional decline of Slovakia’s inland consump-
tion of natural gas decline (in GWh) as compared to 
the electricity available for final consumption from 
2021 to 2022:
= – 9 500 – (– 4 000)
≈ – 5 500
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is essential to convert the order of magnitude and 
units of the inland consumption of natural gas to 
find the difference. It is convenient to approximate 
the differences in order to quickly find an estimate 
which is close enough for us to guess the correct 
option. Be careful to subtract the 2021 figures from 
the figures of 2022 for both calculations.

questions.question: In 2022, what is the increase required in the inland consumption of natural gas in Estonia to match the electricity available for final consumption (in Terajoules)? (1 Terajoule = 1 000 Gigajoules)
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the inland consumption 
of natural gas and electricity available for final 
consumption in Estonia in 2022. We must then 
convert them to common units (Gigajoules). Finally, 

205
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
we can find the difference and convert the solu-
tion to Terajoules by using the provided order of 
magnitude.
Calculation
In 2022, Estonia’s inland consumption of natural gas 
was 13.6 million Gigajoules and electricity available 
for final consumption was 8 348 GWh.
Electricity available for final consumption in Estonia 
in 2022 (in Gigajoules):
= 8 348 * 3 600
≈ 30 000 000
The increase required in the inland consumption of 
natural gas in Estonia to match the electricity avail-
able for final consumption (in Gigajoules):
= 30 000 000 – (13.6 * 1 000 000)
= 30 000 000 – 13 600 000
= 16 400 000
We know that:
1 Terajoule = 1 000 Gigajoules
The increase required in the inland consumption of 
natural gas in Estonia to match the electricity avail-
able for final consumption (in Terajoules):
= 16 400 000 / 1 000
= 16 400
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You need to deal with one unit conversion, and two 
conversions of order of magnitude. If you are not 
careful with the conversions, you might end up with 
a solution that matches an incorrect answer like 
option B. The key thing to get right is the accuracy 
of the order of magnitude and the correct units of 
the solution. The approximations of numbers, used 
for quicker calculations, will eventually not matter 
as far as choosing the correct answer is concerned.
You can also see that options C and E are basically 
the same number with a difference in the order of 
magnitude. If you need to make a pure guess, this is 
an indication that either of these would likely make 
a good choice for the guess.

questions.question: What was the total population of Croatia in 2018?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must determine the relationship between 
Generation of plastic packaging waste per capita 
and Generation of plastic packaging waste and then 
use this relationship to determine Croatia’s total 
population in 2018.
Calculation
We know that:
Generation of plastic packaging waste per capita, in 
kgs:
= ((Generation of plastic packaging waste, in millions of 
kgs) * (1 000 000)) / (Total population)
Hence:
Total population:
= ((Generation of plastic packaging waste, in millions 
of kgs) * (1 000 000)) / (Generation of plastic packaging 
waste per capita, in kgs)
Total population of Croatia in 2018:
(64.58 * 1 000 000) / 15.73
≈ 4.105 * 1 000 000
≈ 4 105 000
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Approximating the division result to three decimal 
places is good enough to arrive at the correct solu-
tion quickly. Make sure that you select the data for 
the correct country because Iceland’s total popu-
lation for 2018 has been provided as an incorrect 
option (option A). 
We know that 65 million divided by 15 results in a 
value somewhere between 4 and 5 million. Hence, 
options B, C and D should be the only candidates for 
a guess, if required.

questions.question: Had Iceland’s population remained unchanged from 2016 to 2020 and the generation of plastic packaging waste per capita stayed at the given levels, what would have been the generation of plastic packaging waste in Iceland in 2020 (in millions of kgs)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the total population of 
Iceland in 2016 by using the per capita formula. Then 
we must use the per capita figure of 2020 along with 
the total population of 2016 to compute the genera-
tion of plastic packaging waste in 2020.

206
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Calculation
We know that:
Total population:
= ((Generation of plastic packaging waste, in millions 
of kgs) * (1 000 000)) / (Generation of plastic packaging 
waste per capita, in kgs)
Total population of Iceland in 2016:
= (15.31 * 1 000 000) / 46.04
≈ (1 * 1 000 000) / 3
≈ 333 333
By rearranging the per capita formula, we get:
Generation of plastic packaging waste, in millions of 
kgs:
= ((Total population) * (Generation of plastic packaging 
waste per capita, in kgs)) / 1 000 000
Generation of plastic packaging waste in Iceland in 
2020 if the total population has not changed since 
2016:
= (333 333 * 39.42) / 1 000 000
≈ (333 333 * 3 * 13) / 1 000 000
= (1 000 000 * 13) / 1 000 000
≈ 13 (million kg)
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The approximations made in the calculation made 
it quicker and convenient. The approximate result 
was close enough to option A. Option E provides 
the correct solution but in kgs, whereas the solution 
is required in millions of kgs. Hence, you must keep 
note of the order of magnitude.
If you need to make a guess, you might notice that 
Options B, C and D are equal to the actual genera-
tion of plastic packaging waste in the three years in 
the table. It is unlikely that the calculated solution 
would coincidentally be equal to an actual value. 
Hence these three options can be ruled out.

questions.question: In 2020, Malta aimed to reduce its annual generation of plastic packaging waste to 13 million kgs by the year 2022. The projected population increase in Malta by 2022 was 12% from 2020. What was the projected generation of plastic packaging waste per capita for 2022 (in kgs) assuming the target was achieved?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first calculate the total population of Malta 
in 2020. We then calculate the projected population 
of Malta in 2022 by using the projected percentage 
increase and then use it with the target generation 
of plastic packaging waste for 2022 to determine the 
projected per capita figure for 2022.
Calculation
We know that:
Total population:
= ((Generation of plastic packaging waste, in millions 
of kgs) * (1 000 000)) / (Generation of plastic packaging 
waste per capita, in kgs)
The total population of Malta in 2020:
= (14.11 * 1 000 000) / 27.43
≈ (14 * 1 000 000) / 28
= 1 000 000 / 2
= 500 000
The projected population of Malta in 2022:
= (Population in 2020) * (100% + 12%)
= 500 000 * (1.12)
= 560 000
The projected generation of plastic packaging waste 
per capita (in kgs) for 2022:
= ((Generation of plastic packaging waste, in millions of 
kgs) * (1 000 000)) / Total population
= (13 * 1 000 000) / 560 000
≈ 23.2
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The approximate solution value lies between the 
values of options C and D. But option D is simply 
the per capita figure for 2020 reduced by 12%, which 
is obviously incorrect. Hence, option C must be 
correct. 
The population of Malta was projected to increase 
while the total generation of plastic packaging waste 
was projected to decrease by 2022. This means that 
the per capita quantity for 2022 would be lower 
than that of 2020. Hence, option E cannot be correct. 
Option A is simply a linear extrapolation of the trend 
between 2018 and 2020.
Be careful to read the data for the correct year and 
the correct country. As the two previous questions 
referred to Croatia and Ireland you have to mentally 
switch country each time.

questions.question: What was the difference in the HPI values o USA and Belgium in Q4/2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must find the HPI values for USA and Belgium 
for Q4/2022, based on the given values for Q1/2023 

207
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
and the percentage changes since. Then, we can find 
the difference between those values.
Calculation
We know that:
HPI in Q1/2023 = (HPI in Q4/2022) * (100% + 
(Percentage change since Q4/2022))
HPI value for USA in Q4/2022:
216.64 = (HPI in Q4/2022) * (100% + 0.36%)
217 ≈ (HPI in Q4/2022) * 100%
HPI in Q4/2022 ≈ 217
HPI value for Belgium in Q4/2022:
149.95 = (HPI in Q4/2022) * (100% + 1.50%)
150 ≈ (HPI in Q4/2022) * 101.50%
150 = (HPI in Q4/2022) * 1.015
HPI in Q4/2022 = 150 / 1.015
HPI in Q4/2022 ≈ 147.75
The difference in the HPI values of USA and Belgium 
in Q4/2022:
= 217 – 147.75
= 69.25
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is very important to note that the percentage 
change is from Q4/2022 to Q1/2023 and not the 
other way around. This means that the given values 
of Q1/2023 are the result of the increase.
What if you need to guess? You can see from the table 
that Belgium’s HPI value increased slightly more 
than that of the USA from Q4/2022 to Q1/2023. The 
difference in Q4/2022 was 66.69 so the figure we are 
looking for should also be slightly more than 66.69. 
This means that Options C and D are the only possi-
bilities, so you are left with a 50/50 choice. To be 
sure which one is right, you would have to do the 
calculation. 
In fact, if you use the percentage increases since 
Q1/2022 by mistake, you get option C. Option A is 
the difference between the percentage changes for 
Belgium and the USA in Q4/2022. Option B is the 
difference between the HPI values for the USA and 
Belgium in Q1/2023. Option E is the HPI value for 
Belgium in Q4/2022 and not the difference between 
the values for the USA and Belgium.

questions.question: What was the percentage change in the HPI value of New Zealand from Q1/2022 to Q4/2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the HPI values in New 
Zealand for Q1/2022 and Q4/2022 and then calcu-
late the percentage change.
Calculation
We know that:
HPI in Q1/2023 = (HPI in Q4/2022) * (100% + 
(Percentage change since Q4/2022))
HPI value for New Zealand in Q4/2022:
232.71 = (HPI in Q4/2022) * (100% – 2.59%)
233 ≈ (HPI in Q4/2022) * 97.5%
(HPI in Q4/2022) = 233 / 0.975
(HPI in Q4/2022) ≈ 233 * 1.025 = 233 * 1 + 233 * 0.025
(HPI in Q4/2022) ≈ 233 + 6
HPI in Q4/2022 ≈ 239
Similarly, we have:
HPI in Q1/2023 = (HPI in Q1/2022) * (100% + 
(Percentage change since Q1/2022))
HPI value for New Zealand in Q1/2022:
232.71 = (HPI in Q1/2022) * (100% – 11.70%)
233 ≈ (HPI in Q1/2022) * 88%
233 = (HPI in Q1/2022) * 0.88
HPI in Q1/2022 = 233 / 0.88
HPI in Q1/2022 ≈ 265
The percentage change in the HPI value of New 
Zealand from Q1/2022 to Q4/2022:
= ((239 – 265) / 265) * 100%
= (– 26 / 265) * 100%
≈ – 10%
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
We have made quite a few calculations to get to this 
point but if you look at the answer options there are 
shortcuts that will avoid doing any significant calcu-
lations at all. 
The percentage decrease from Q1/2022 to Q1/2023 
was much higher than that from Q4/2022 to 
Q1/2023. Hence, the HPI value for Q1/2022 will be 
much higher as compared to Q4/2023. This means 
that the percentage change must be negative. This 
conclusion eliminates options D and E. Option A is 
the sum of the percentage changes from Q1/2022 to 
Q1/2023 and from Q4/2022 to Q1/2023 while option 

208
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
C is the difference of the same percentage changes. 
Since the given percentage changes are not relative 
to the same HPI value, simple addition and subtrac-
tion of percentage changes will not suffice. Option B 
is therefore the only realistic choice.

questions.question: Assuming linear growth since Q1/2022, wha is the anticipated HPI value for Türkiye in Q1/2024?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the HPI value in Türkiye 
for Q1/2022. Then using a linear model, calculate an 
estimate for the HPI value in Q1/2024.
Calculation
We know that:
HPI in Q1/2023 = (HPI in Q1/2022) * (100% + 
(Percentage change since Q1/2022))
HPI value for Türkiye in Q1/2022:
1 726.23 = (HPI in Q1/2022) * (100% + 132.84%)
1 729 ≈ (HPI in Q1/2022) * 233.33%
1 729 = (HPI in Q1/2022) * 2.3333
1 729 = (HPI in Q1/2022) * 7/3
HPI in Q1/2022 = 1 729 * 3 / 7
HPI in Q1/2022 ≈ 741
Therefore, assuming a linear model, the change in 
one year from Q1/2022 to Q1/2023 will be equal to 
the change in the one year from Q1/2023 to Q1/2024. 
Hence, the anticipated HPI value for Türkiye in 
Q1/2024 is:
1 726.23 + (1 726.23 – 741)
1 726.23 + 985.23
= 2 711
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Thinking about the answer options can save you 
from doing a lot of calculations. The 132.84% 
increase from Q1/2022 to Q1/2023 shows that the 
value in Q1/2022 was less than half of the value in 
Q1/2023. Hence, options A and B can be ruled out. 
Option C is simply the sum of the values in Q1/2022 
and Q1/2023, which is not linear. Option E is equal 
to the value of Q1/2023 increased by 132.84%, which 
is incorrect. That leaves only D.
The value for Q4/2022 has nothing to do with the 
linear extrapolation since we are basing the increase 
in value on the trend between Q1/2022 and Q1/2023.

questions.question: What was the average speed of the aircraft for the complete flight (in mph)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the formula for average speed and the 
actual travelled miles to calculate the average speed 
of the aircraft for the complete flight.
Calculation
We know that:
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
The average speed of the aircraft for the complete 
flight (in mph):
= 838 / (1 + (55/60))
≈ 840 / (1 + 0.9) 
= 840 / 1.9
≈ 442
Option D is the closest in value, hence it must be 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is very important to convert the time to hours by 
dividing the minutes by 60. Since the option values 
are close together, you cannot in this case approxi-
mate the figures too much. If you do, your solution 
might match options A or B. You must use the actual 
distance travelled for the calculation of the average 
speed as opposed to the direct distance. If you do 
the latter, you will get incorrect option C.
Option E is obviously a totally unrealistic speed for 
an ordinary commercial flight from the UK to Spain. 
However, while the flight details in this case imme-
diately tell you that E is wrong, it is worth remem-
bering that the scenarios in numerical reasoning 
questions, while highly likely to be realistic, do not 
necessarily have to be.

questions.question: Had the flight taken a direct route and flown at a 2% faster average speed, how much flight time would have been saved (in minutes)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first determine the suggested faster speed, 
based on the average speed of the actual flight, and 
then used the direct distance provided to calculate 
the minutes that would have been saved.

209
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Calculation
We know that:
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
2% faster average speed (in mph) as compared to the 
actual flight’s average speed:
(838 / (1 + (55/60))) * (100% + 2%)
≈ (838 / 1.9) * (100% + 2%)
≈ 437.2 * 102%
≈ 437.2 + 4.4 + 4.4
≈ 446
By re-arranging the average speed formula, we get:
Total time = Total distance / Average speed
Time taken for the flight with 2% faster average 
speed and a direct route:
814 / 446
≈ 1.83 hours
≈ 1 hour + (0.83 * 60) minutes
≈ 1 hour + 50 minutes
Minutes saved with 2% faster average speed and a 
direct route:
(1 hour 55 minutes) – (1 hour 50 minutes)
= 5 minutes
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
We could have approximated the actual flight’s 
average speed as we did before, but since we know 
the exact figure from the option of the first question, 
we could use that for higher accuracy. If the average 
speed was reduced by 2%, by mistake, the result 
would equal option B. 
You must of course take note that time will be saved 
on two counts. Firstly, due to the higher average 
speed, i.e., more miles covered per unit of time. 
Secondly, due to the shorter distance that needs to 
be covered, i.e., 838 – 814 = 24 miles less. You must 
use the direct distance. If you use the distance of the 
actual flight, you will end up with answer option C.

questions.question: Another aircraft took off 24 hours later from EMA for BCN and maintained the same average speed as flight 9388, but had to go around a major storm system on the way. How much more distance was travelled by the second aircraft, as a percentage of flight 9388’s distance, if it reached Barcelona in 2 hours and 18 minutes?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first calculate flight 9388’s average speed 
and then use it along with the time provided for the 
second aircraft to calculate the distance travelled by 
it. Finally, we will calculate the new distance trav-
elled as a percentage of the original.
Calculation
We know that:
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
The average speed of the aircraft for the complete 
flight 9388 (in mph):
= 838 / (1 + (55/60))
= 437.2
By re-arranging the average speed formula, we get:
Total distance = Average speed * Total time
Distance travelled by the second aircraft (in miles):
437.2 * (2 + (18/60))
= 437.2 * (2.3) 
≈ 1 005
Additional distance travelled by the second aircraft 
as a percentage of the distance travelled by flight 
9388:
((1 005 – 838) / 838) * 100%
= (167 / 838) * 100%
≈ 20%
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
A much quicker shortcut would be to find the ratio 
of the two flight times, i.e., (2 hours 18 minutes) / 
(1 hour 55 minutes) = 1.20. Hence, the ratio of the 
distances will also be the same at the same average 
speed.

questions.question: If the median Hong Kong property price in 2023 (mid-year) was 17.9 million Hong Kong Dollars (HKD), what was the corresponding mortgage value (in HKD)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the price to income ratio and the given median 
property price to evaluate the annual income per 
capita for Hong Kong. Use this result to calculate the 
corresponding mortgage value by using the given 
mortgage as a % of annual income.
Calculation
We know that:
Price to income ratio:
= Median property price / Annual income per capita
Hence:
Annual income per capita:
= Median property price/ Price to income ratio

210
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Annual income per capita in Hong Kong in 2023 
mid-year (in HKD): 
= (17.9 * 1 000 000) / 42.1
≈ (18 / 42) * 1 000 000
= (3 / 7) * 1 000 000
We also know that:
Mortgage as a % of annual income:
= (Mortgage value / Annual income per capita) * 100%
Hence:
Mortgage value:
= ((Mortgage as a % of annual income) * (Annual 
income per capita)) / 100%
The corresponding mortgage value based on the 
median property price in Hong Kong in 2023 mid-
year (in HKD):
= (275.9% / 100%) * ((3 / 7) * 1 000 000)
≈ 2.8 * (3 / 7) * 1 000 000
= 0.4 * 3 * 1 000 000
= 1 200 000
Option C is the closest value to our estimate and is 
therefore the option to choose.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
There are a lot of columns in this table so it’s easy to 
get lost. It is very important not to confuse the price 
to income ratio with the price to rent ratio. You would 
end with option B if you made that mistake. It is also 
crucial not to confuse the annual income per capita 
with the total annual rental income.

questions.question: What was the difference between the Price to Rent ratios of Indonesia’s City Centre areas and Outside of City Centre areas?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the given gross rental yield % to calculate the 
price to rent ratio in Indonesia’s city centre areas. 
Then use the result to find the difference between 
the price to rent ratios of city centre and outside city 
centre areas.
Calculation
We know that:
Gross Rental Yield (%):
= ((Total annual rental income) / (Property price)) * 
100%
Hence:
Price to Rent ratio:
= (Property price) / (Total annual rental income)
= 100% / Gross Rental Yield (%)
The price to rent ratio in Indonesia’s city centre areas 
in 2023 mid-year:
= 100% / 4.5%
= 100 / 4.5
= 1 000 / 45
= 200 / 9
≈ 22
The difference between the price to rent ratios of city 
centre and outside city centre areas in Indonesia in 
2023 mid-year:
= 22 – 19.2
= 2.8
Option B is the closest in value. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You have just done a question about Hong Kong. 
This one is about Indonesia so, as always, make sure 
you are answering the right question! If you use the 
data for Hong Kong, you could end up mistakenly 
choosing option E.

questions.question: A property owner is earning an annual rental income of 20 million Pakistani Rupees (PKR) from a property located outside of city centre. How much more, or less, could he earn in rental income (in PKR), annually, from selling the existing property, purchasing a new property in the city centre (having the same price as the property sold) and then renting it out?

options.text: No change in annual rental income

questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first find the property price of the existing 
property by using the price to rent ratio (outside city 
centre) and the given annual rental income. Then 
we must use the price to rent ratio (city centre) to 
determine the annual rental income from the new 
property in the city centre.
Calculation
We know that:
Price to Rent ratio:
= (Property price) / (Total annual rental income)
Hence:
Property price = (Price to rent ratio) * (Total annual 
rental income)
Property price of the existing property outside city 
centre (in PKR):
= 19.8 * 20 * 1 000 000

211
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
We will simplify to:
≈ 20 * 20 * 1 000 000
= 400 000 000
By re-arranging the price to rent ratio formula we 
get:
Total annual rental income = (Property price) / (Price to 
rent ratio)
Total annual rental income expected from the new 
property purchased in city centre (in PKR): 
= 400 000 000 / 21.1
≈ 400 000 000 / 21
= 400 000 000 / (10 * 2.1)
= 40 000 000 / 2.1
≈ 19 000 000
The annual rental income from the new property 
will be less (in PKR) by:
= 20 000 000 – 19 000 000
= 1 000 000
This figure corresponds exactly with option B. But does 
this mean option B is the correct answer? Remember 
that we simplified the actual figures to make our esti-
mate easier, so although option B is an exact match 
with our estimate, we know that the actual solution 
cannot be equal to it. In fact, option A is correct as the 
only other figure showing a drop in income. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The data approximations helped us get a fairly quick 
estimate of the change in annual rental income, but 
an alternative approach would be to think about the 
answer options before doing any calculations. 
Consider the price to rent ratios in Pakistan. The 
properties in city centre areas are more expensive 
relative to the rent they earn compared with the 
outside of city centre areas. Hence, selling the outside 
of centre area property and buying a city centre area 
property will result in a loss of annual rental income. 
This means that only options A and B can possibly 
be correct. Moreover, the numbers of Pakistan’s price 
to rent ratios are not round enough to make a result 
like option B likely. Option A is the best choice for an 
answer without any calculations at all.

questions.question: How much did Germany export in 2022 (in millions of euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the GDP formula, the given GDP value and 
the given values of the remaining components to 
compute the value of exports by Germany in 2022. 
We will use rounding of figures where possible.
Calculation
We know that:
GDP = (Consumption) + (Capital formation) + (Exports 
– Imports)
Hence:
Exports = (GDP) – (Consumption) – (Capital formation) 
+ (Imports)
Exports = 3 876 810 – 2 830 218 – 970 315 + 1 897 947
Exports ≈ 3 900 000 – 2 800 000 – 1 000 000 + 1 900 
000
Exports = 2 000 000 million euros 
Option E is the closest to our approximation. Hence, 
it is the best choice.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Be careful to use the data from 2022. If you use the 
GDP for 2020 or 2021 you will likely end up with 
options A or B. Likewise, if you don’t form the equa-
tion properly, you might end up with options C or D.

questions.question: Given that, in 2020, the sum of Italy’s consumption expenditure and capital formation was 1 601 235 million euros, what were its net exports (in millions of euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the GDP formula along with the Net 
Exports formula and the given GDP and its compo-
nent values to calculate Net Exports by Italy in 2020, 
again using rounding.
Calculation
We know that:
GDP = (Consumption) + (Capital formation) + (Exports 
– Imports)
Net Exports = Exports – Imports

212
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Hence:
GDP = (Consumption) + (Capital formation) + (Net 
Exports)
Net Exports = (GDP) – (Consumption + Capital 
formation)
Net Exports = (1 661 020) – (1 601 235)
Net Exports ≈ 1 660 000 – 1 600 000
Net Exports = +60 000 million Euros
Option B is closest in value to our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Although the eventual calculation involved the 
simple subtraction of two numbers, the correct 
manipulation of the given equations is the key to 
solving this question. You must be careful also to 
use Italy’s GDP value for 2020. If you used the values 
for other years, you would end up with options C 
or D.
Since the sum of the two components is less than the 
GDP for that year, it is obvious that the net exports 
will be positive. Therefore, option A can be ruled out 
immediately.

questions.question: Given that consumption, capital formation and net exports of Spain experienced the same percentage growth as the GDP from 2021 to 2022, what was Spain’s approximate consumption expenditure in 2021 (in millions of euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the GDP formula to determine Spain’s consump-
tion expenditure in 2022. Then use the GDP growth 
percentage from 2021 to 2022 to determine its esti-
mated consumption expenditure in 2021. We will 
use rounding to speed up the calculation.
Calculation
We know that:
GDP = (Consumption) + (Capital formation) + (Exports) 
– (Imports)
Hence:
Consumption = (GDP) – (Capital formation) – (Exports) 
+ (Imports)
Consumption = 1 327 108 – 279 087 – 551 431 + 532 
726
Consumption ≈ 1 330 000 – 280 000 – 550 000 + 535 
000
Consumption = 1 035 000 million euros (in 2022)
Spain’s GDP growth factor from 2021 to 2022:
= 1 327 108 / 1 206 842
≈ 1 350 000 / 1 200 000
= 1 350 / 1 200
= 270 / 240
= 9 / 8
Spain’s estimated consumption expenditure for 
2021:
= (Spain’s consumption expenditure in 2022) / (Spain’s 
GDP growth factor from 2021 to 2022)
= 1 035 000 / (9 / 8)
= 1 035 000 * (8 / 9)
= 115 000 * 8
= 920 000 million euros
Option B is the closest to our approximation. Hence, 
it is probably correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Since Spain’s GDP of 2022 is higher than that in 2021, 
and consumption had the same percentage growth 
as GDP, it is obvious that the consumption expendi-
ture of 2021 is going to be lower than that in 2022. 
Hence, options D and E can be ruled out.

questions.question: What was Sweden’s gross debt of househol (in millions of euros) in 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use Sweden’s gross debt-to-gross dispos-
able income ratio of 2021 along with Sweden’s gross 
disposable income in 2021 to calculate the gross debt 
of households for the same year.
Calculation
We know that:
Gross debt to gross disposable income ratio:
= (Gross debt of households) / (Gross disposable 
income)
Hence:
Gross debt of households:
= (Gross debt to gross disposable income ratio) * (Gross 
disposable income)
Gross debt of households in Sweden in 2021 (in 
millions of euros):
= 172.04% * 550 665 
= 1.7204 * 550 665
= 947 364
Option E is therefore correct.

213
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Since the gross debt-to-gross disposable income for 
households is greater than 100%, our solution must 
be greater in value than the gross disposable income 
for Sweden in the given year. Hence, options A and 
B can be eliminated without any calculations.
This leave options, C, D and E. You should always 
look for opportunities for rounding numbers but 
must be careful when the likely answer options are 
close in value, as is the case here with C, D and E. 
Your level of accuracy may not be sufficient. In this 
case, if we had rounded 1.7204 to 1.7 and 550 655 to 
550 000, we would have calculated 935 000, which is 
the incorrect answer option D.
When using bar charts, take particular care that you 
select the figures for the correct year and for the 
correct country. If you use the wrong year or country, 
you will end up with answer options A or C. Using 
the ratio incorrectly can end up with option B.

questions.question: Given that the gross disposable income of households of Denmark was 390 000 million euros in 2022, what is the best estimate of th ds n he gross debt of households of Denmark in 2022 (in millions of euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first estimate the gross debt-to-gross 
disposable income of households’ ratio of Denmark 
for 2022 based on the trend from 2019 to 2021. We 
must then use the given gross disposable income for 
2022 to estimate the gross debt of Denmark house-
holds in 2022. 
Calculation
Change in the gross debt-to-gross disposable income 
of households’ ratio of Denmark from 2019 to 2021:
= 205.58% – 211.19%
≈ –5.6%
Estimated annual change in the gross debt-to-gross 
disposable income of households’ ratio of Denmark 
from 2019 to 2021:
= –5.6% / 2 = –2.8%
Estimated gross debt-to-gross disposable income of 
households’ ratio of Denmark in 2022:
= (Gross debt to gross disposable income of households’ 
ratio of Denmark in 2022) + (Estimated annual change in 
the ratio of Denmark from 2019 to 2021)
= 205.58% –2.8%
= 203.78%
We know that:
Gross debt of households:
= (Gross debt to gross disposable income ratio) * (Gross 
disposable income)
Estimated gross debt of households in Denmark in 
2022 (in millions of Euros):
= 203.78% * 390 000
= 2.0378 * 390 000
≈ 2.04 * 390 000
= 795 600
Option C is closest to our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
