In the previous question we did not round numbers 
because the three possible answer options were 
rather close in value. In this case the gaps in values 
are larger, so some rounding to produce an estimate 
was sufficiently accurate.
You must calculate the ratio for 2022 based on linear 
extrapolation from 2019 to 2021. But do remember to 
divide the change in the ratio in two years by 2, since 
we are only estimating for 1 year ahead of 2021.

questions.question: The gross debt in which of these countries had a lower growth rate than that of the gross disposable income from 2017 to 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must study the formula for gross debt-to-gross 
disposable income ratio and then use it to analyze 
which of these countries had a lower growth rate 
than that of the gross disposable income from 2017 
to 2021.
Calculation
We know that:
Gross debt to gross disposable income ratio:
= (Gross debt of households) / (Gross disposable 
income)
A lower growth rate of gross debt as compared to the 
gross disposable income from 2017 to 2021 indicates 
that the numerator, in the above-noted formula, 
grows by a smaller percentage than the denominator. 
This results in the ratio decreasing in value. Hence, 
we need to look at countries which had a decreasing 
gross debt-to-gross disposable income ratio from 
2017 to 2021.

214
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Gross debt-to-gross disposable income ratios from 
2017 to 2021:
Denmark: Decreased from 216.19% to 205.58%
Finland: Increased from 112.78% to 118.26%
Latvia: Decreased from 34.79% to 29.18%
Sweden: Increased from 162.46% to 172.04%
Therefore, Denmark and Latvia had a lower growth 
rate of gross debt than that of gross disposable 
income from 2017 to 2021. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The key to this question is understanding what you 
are being asked and how to use the formula. It does 
not involve any calculations. 
You must be careful that you read the bar chart 
correctly because the data for 2017 is shown in the 
bars at the bottom while that for 2021 is shown in the 
bars at the top, i.e. possibly the reverse of what you 
might be expecting. You must also check for each 
country. Options A and C are incorrect because they 
exclude the other country.

questions.question: What percentage of worldwide vegetable oil consumption did sunflower seed oil, palm kernel oil and peanut oil account for in 2019/20?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must total the consumption of sunflower seed 
oil, palm kernel oil and peanut oil and divide it by 
the total consumption of vegetable oils worldwide in 
2019/20 to find the percentage.
Calculation
Total consumption of vegetable oil worldwide in 
2019/20 (in million metric tons):
≈ 200
Sunflower seed oil, palm kernel oil and peanut oil 
accounted for this percentage of the worldwide 
vegetable oil consumption in 2019/20:
= ((18.92 + 8.15 + 6.22) / 200) * 100%
≈ (33.29 / 200) * 100%
≈ 16.645%
Our calculation clearly most closely corresponds to 
Option B. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must read the data for the correct year out of 
the given data for 10 years. For example, option A 
provides the percentage for 2015/16. Palm kernel oil 
must not be confused with Palm oil. If you make 
that mistake, your solution might match option D.

questions.question: It is estimated that about 90% of palm oil is used for food consumption, whereas industrial consumption claims the remaining 10%. What was the increase in the worldwide food consumption of palm oil as a percentage of worldwide vegetable oil consumption from 2015/16 to 2018/19?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the use of palm oil used for food 
consumption as a percentage of the worldwide vege-
table oil consumption for 2015/16 and 2018/19. We can 
then find the difference between the two percentages.
Calculation
Palm oil consumed in 2015/16 (in million metric 
tons): 
= 59.38 ≈ 60
Palm oil used for food consumption in 2015/16 (in 
million metric tons): 
= 60 * 90% = 54
Worldwide vegetable oil consumption in 2015/16 (in 
million metric tons): 
≈ 180
Use of palm oil for food consumption as a percentage 
of worldwide vegetable oil consumption in 2015/16:
= (54 / 180) * 100%
= 30%
Palm oil consumed in 2018/19 (in million metric 
tons): 
70.94 ≈ 71
Palm oil used for food consumption in 2018/19 (in 
million metric tons): 
= 71 * 90% = 64
Worldwide vegetable oil consumption in 2018/19 (in 
million metric tons): 
≈ 198
Use of palm oil for food consumption as a percentage 
of worldwide vegetable oil consumption in 2018/19:
= (64 / 198) * 100%
≈ 32.3%

215
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Increase in the use of palm oil for food consumption 
as a percentage of worldwide vegetable oil consump-
tion from 2015/16 to 2018/19:
= 32.3% – 30%
= 2.3%
Option B is the closest in value. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Bar charts will often give a visual clue to the answer 
without calculations. A quick look at the chart in 
this case tells us that answer options C, D and E are 
far too high because the upward steps in consump-
tion of palm oil are fairly similar to the upward 
steps in total consumption of all vegetable oils. The 
increase in the share of palm oil can only be very 
small. Thus, if you have to guess, the choice is only 
between A and B. To be sure of your answer, you 
would have to do a calculation. However as 2.2% is 
11 times greater than 0.2% it was safe, as done above, 
to do some rounding of numbers. 
Option C gives the percentage increase in the 
percentages, which is not required. 
Options D and E give the use of palm oil for food 
consumption as a percentage of worldwide vege-
table oil consumption for 2015/16 and 2018/19 
respectively.

questions.question: Global vegetable oil production amounted to around 208.81 million metric tons in 2021/22. Assuming that vegetable oil was produced in the same proportion as it was consumed, how much more soybean oil was produced worldwide in 2021/22 as compared to palm kernel oil (in million metric tons)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first find the difference in soybean oil 
consumption and palm kernel oil consumption in 
2021/22. We must then multiply it by the worldwide 
vegetable oil production to consumption ratio to find 
how much more soybean oil was produced world-
wide in 2021/22 as compared to palm kernel oil.
Calculation
Worldwide soybean oil consumption in 2021/22 (in 
million metric tons) = 59.30
Worldwide palm kernel oil consumption in 2021/22 
(in million metric tons) = 8.29
Difference in the worldwide soybean and palm 
kernel oil consumption in 2021/22 (in million metric 
tons):
= 59.30 – 8.29 ≈ 51
Worldwide vegetable oil production to consumption 
ratio in 2021/22:
= (Worldwide vegetable oil production) / (Worldwide 
vegetable oil consumption)
≈ 208.81 / 205 ≈ 1.02
Difference between the production of soybean oil 
and palm kernel oil in 2021/22 (in million metric 
tons):
= ((Worldwide vegetable oil production) / (Worldwide 
vegetable oil consumption)) * (Difference in 
consumption of soybean and palm kernel oil)
= 1.02 * 51
= 1 * 51 + 0.02 * 51
≈ 51 + 1
≈ 52
Based on the approximate calculations, option D is 
the closest in value. Hence, it is probably correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Some approximating of numbers helped us to do 
the calculations faster. As options C and D are fairly 
close, rounding our numbers was not entirely risk-
free but the answer we got has sufficient confidence 
for an exam with severe time pressures.
It is always easy to get lost where charts have a lot 
of data. There are 10 columns of data here, and you 
have to be careful to use the figures for 2021/22. 
Likewise, for palm kernel oil and not palm oil. 
Option B is there as a trap if such a mistake is made. 
Options A and E provide the consumption of palm 
kernel oil and soybean oil in 2021/22 respectively. 
Option C provides the difference between the two.

questions.question: Assuming constant land area, what is Indonesia’s projected population density (p square km) for 2050?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use Indonesia’s projected population in 
2050 along with its given land area to calculate its 
projected population density (per square km) for 
2050.
Calculation
Indonesia’s projected population for 2050 is 0.317 
billion.
Indonesia’s land area is 1.9 million square km.
Indonesia’s projected population density (per square 
km) for 2050:

216
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
= Indonesia’s projected population for 2025 / 
Indonesia’s land area
= (0.317 * 1 000 000 000) / (1.9 * 1 000 000)
= (0.317 * 1 000) / 1.9
≈ 320 / 2
= 160
Based on our approximate calculation, option D is 
the correct answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The fairly wide spread of values in the answer 
options indicated that some rounding of figures 
would be a safe option. 
It is essential to note that the population is in billions 
(which has nine zeroes) and the land area is given in 
millions (having six zeroes). The population density 
divides the population by the land area. Hence, nine 
zeroes are in the numerator and six zeroes are in the 
denominator. If you make a mistake in this regard, 
options A and B might match your incorrect solution.
The population is expected to grow from Sep-2023 
to 2050 but with a constant land area. So, it is logical 
to expect a number higher than 148 (the density in 
Sep-2023) in 2050. Options D and E are hence the 
only two reasonable choices for a guess.

questions.question: Given that the Earth’s total land area is approximately 148 940 thousand square km, what was the average land area per person o the world in Sep-2023 (in square km)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Initially, we must calculate the world population in 
Sep-2023 by using any given country’s population 
and its percentage share of the world population. 
We must then divide the given land area of Earth by 
the calculated population.
Calculation
Let w, be the world population in Sep-2023 (in 
billions).
India’s population Sep-2023 was 1.432 billion which 
contributed a 17.85% share to the world population:
(1.432 / w) * 100% = 17.85%
(1.432 / w) = (17.85% / 100%) = 0.1785
0.1785w = 1.432
w = 1.432 / 0.1785
w ≈ 1.45 / 0.18 ≈ 8
The average land area per person of the world in 
Sep-2023 (in square km):
= (148 940 * 1 000) / (8 * 1 000 000 000)
= (0.14894 * 1 000 000 * 1 000) / (8 * 1 000 000 000)
= 0.14894 / 8
≈ 0.15 / 8
≈ 0.02
Option B is the closest to our estimated value.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The wide spread of values in the answer options 
ensured that approximating the numbers would 
produce a correct result. Getting the order of magni-
tude right is the crucial part. The land area is given 
in thousands whereas the population figures are in 
billions. 
Note also that the solution required is not the popu-
lation density. In fact, it is the reciprocal of popula-
tion density. If the population density is calculated 
instead, your answer might match the incorrect 
options A or D.

questions.question: Assuming constant growth rates, what will be the difference between Bangladesh’s population and Russia’s population (in millions) in Sep-2027?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must apply the given growth rates to the popula-
tions of the two countries in Sep-2023 for 4 consec-
utive years to calculate the respective projected 
populations in Sep-2027. Then we can find the 
difference between the two projected populations.
Calculation
In Sep-2023, Bangladesh’s population was 0.173 
billion whereas Russia’s population was 0.144 billion 
and the respective growth rates were 1.03% and 
–0.19%.
The projected population of Bangladesh after 4 years 
in Sep-2027 (in billions):
0.173 * (100% + 1.03%) * (100% + 1.03%) * (100% + 
1.03%) * (100% + 1.03%) 
= 0.173 * (101.03%)⁴
= 0.173 * (1.0103)⁴
≈ 0.173 * 1.04
≈ 0.18
Similarly, the projected population of Russia after 4 
years in Sep-2027 (in billions):
= 0.144 * (100% – 0.19%)⁴
= 0.144 * (99.81%)⁴
= 0.144 * (0.9981) ⁴

217
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
≈ 0.14 * 0.992
≈ 0.139
The difference between Bangladesh’s population 
and Russia’s population (in millions) in Sep-2027:
= (0.18 – 0.139) * (1 000 000 000 / 1 000 000)
= 0.041 * 1000 = 41
Option D is clearly closest in value to our estimate. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Strictly speaking, you must compound the growth 
rate as shown in the calculations. But since the time 
period for projection is relatively short, i.e., 4 years, 
calculation assuming linear growth should also 
yield a similar result. The critical point is that the 
solution is required in millions, not billions. This 
change in order of magnitude at the last moment can 
easily throw anyone off. In such an event, option B 
will match your solution. 
Option C, and its variant option A, are simply the 
difference in the populations in Sep-2023.
As can be seen from the growth rates, the population 
of Bangladesh is increasing while the population of 
Russia is decreasing. Hence, the gap of 29 million in 
Sep-2023 is bound to increase. Therefore, options D 
and E would be the obvious candidates for a guess 
should there be a need to make one.

questions.question: What was the difference between the total greenhouse gases emissions from production activities in Slovakia and Sweden in 2017 (in million kilograms)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the formula for greenhouse gases 
emissions from production activities per capita to 
calculate the total greenhouse gases emissions from 
production activities in Slovakia and Sweden in 2017 
and then find the difference.
Calculation
We know that:
Greenhouse gases emissions from production activi-
ties in kgs per capita:
= Total greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in kgs / Total population
Hence:
Total greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in kgs:
= Greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in kgs per capita * Total population
Total greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities (in million kilograms) in Slovakia in 2017:
= (6 410.31 * 5 435 343) / 1 000 000
≈ (6 500 * 5 450 000) / 1 000 000
= 6.5 * 5 450
= 35 425
Total greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities (in million kilograms) in Sweden in 2017:
= (4 716.31 * 9 995 153) / 1 000 000
≈ (4 716.3 * 10 000 000) / 1 000 000
= 4 716.3 * 10
= 47 163
The difference between the total greenhouse gases 
emissions from production activities in Slovakia and 
Sweden in 2017 (in million kilograms):
= 47 163 – 35425
= 11 738
Option B has the closest value to our estimate. Hence, 
it must be the correct answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is important to note that the solution is required in 
millions of kilograms. Hence, we must divide by a 
million in our calculations. Options C and D provide 
the respective total gas emissions in Slovakia and 
Sweden in 2017. Options A and E provide the differ-
ences of per capita figures and population figures 
respectively.

questions.question: What is the estimated total of greenhouse gases emissions from production activities in Poland in 2019 (in million kilograms), assuming a linear trend from 2018 to 2020?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first estimate the gas emissions per capita 
in Poland in 2019 based on figures for 2018 and 2020. 
We can then use the per capita formula and the 
given population of Poland in 2019 to estimate the 
total greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in Poland in 2019 (in million kilograms).
Calculation
Greenhouse gases emissions from production activ-
ities per capita in Poland in 2018 were 9 669.40 kilo-
grams and in 2020 were 8 945.24 kilograms.

218
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Assuming a linear decrease over two years, the esti-
mated Greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities per capita in Poland in 2019 (in kilograms) 
would be the average of the 2018 and 220 figures:
(9 669.40 + 8 945.24) / 2
≈ (9 650 * 8 950) / 2
= 18 600 / 2
= 9 300
We know that:
Total greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in kgs:
= Greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in kgs per capita * Total population
The estimated total greenhouse gases emissions from 
production activities in Poland in 2019 (in million 
kilograms):
= (9 300 * 37 972 812) / 1 000 000
≈ (9 300 * 38 * 1 000 000) / 1 000 000
= 9 300 * 38
= 9 300 * 40 – 9 300 * 2
= 372 000 – 18 600
= 353 400
Based on our estimate, option D is the correct answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Our approximations in this case made our calcula-
tions quicker and still produced the right answer. 
Had answer options C or E been very close to D 
this would have carried more risk. Option A is basi-
cally the population (in millions) of Poland in 2019. 
Option B is the estimated per capita figure for 2019. 
Options C and E are the total emissions in 2018 and 
2020.

questions.question: Given that the total of greenhouse gases emissions from production activities in Iceland in 2019 was 920 million kilograms less than that in 2018, what were the greenhouse gases emissions from production activities per capita in 2019 (in kilograms)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first calculate the total greenhouse gases 
emissions from production activities in Iceland in 
2018 and then the total figure for 2019 by using the 
given difference. We can use the per capita formula 
to determine Iceland’s per capita figure for 2019.
Calculation
We know that:
Total greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in kgs:
= Greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in kgs per capita * Total population
The 
total 
greenhouse 
gases 
emissions 
from 
production activities in Iceland in 2018 (in million 
kilograms):
= (19 468.44 * 348 460) / 1 000 000
≈ (19 500 * 0.35 * 1 000 000) / 1 000 000
= 19 500 * 0.35
= 6 825
The total greenhouse gases emissions from produc-
tion activities in Iceland in 2018 (in million kilograms):
= 6 825 – 920 
= 5 905
We know that:
Greenhouse gases emissions from production activities 
in kgs per capita:
= Total greenhouse gases emissions from production 
activities in kgs / Total population
Greenhouse gases emission from production activi-
ties per capita in Iceland in 2019 (in kilograms):
= (5 905 * 1 000 000) / 356 991
≈ (5 900 * 1 000 000) / (35 * 10 000)
= 590 000 / 35
≈ 16 850
Option C is the correct answer based on our 
estimation.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Here you have to work with bits of data from the 
bar chart, the table and the question itself, and for 
different years, so it is easy to get confused. Option 
A is the per capita figure for 2020 while option B is 
920 added to it. In option D, 920 is subtracted from 
the per capita figure for 2018.
Approximating the numbers helped in solving the 
question a bit faster. However, there were still a lot 
of calculations. If you are pressed for time, you could 
instead do some very simple ballpark estimates. 
The population doesn’t change much so can be 
discounted as a factor. The kg emissions per capita 
in Iceland were a fair bit over 19 (thousands) in 2018 
and slightly over 13 (thousands) in 2020. Splitting the 
difference gives you 16 (thousands), and because we 
rounded down more to get the 19 than we did for the 
13, we know the figure will actually be over rather 
than less than 16. As you can see, the only answer 
option that resembles 16 000 is C, which is in fact the 
correct choice.

219
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: What percentage of the non-medical staff on 31 December 2005 were pupils/apprentices?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the number of non-medical staff 
on 31 December 2005 by subtracting the number of 
medical staff from the Staff total. We can then work 
out what percentage of the non-medical staff on 31 
December 2005 were pupils/apprentices.
Calculation
Staff total = Medical staff + Non medical staff
Hence:
Non medical staff = Staff total – Medical staff 
The overall number of non-medical staff on 31 
December 2005:
= 1 063 154 – 131 115
≈ 1 060 000 – 130 000
= 930 000
The percentage of the non-medical staff on 31 
December 2005 who were pupils/apprentices:
= ((Number of pupils/apprentices) / (Total number of 
non medical staff)) * 100%
= (72 330 / 930 000) * 100%
≈ (75 000 / 930 000) * 100%
= ((5 * 5 000) / (62 * 5 000)) * 100%
= (5 / 62) * 100%
≈ 8%
Based on our approximate calculations, option B is 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
There is quite a lot of detail in this table. It is essen-
tial to read the data for the correct year and from the 
columns for 31 December. Option C gives the same 
percentage for 2020.
It is critical also to note that the number is required 
as a percentage of non-medical staff and not the staff 
total. If a mistake is made, option A will match 
your solution. Options D and E give the number of 
medical staff and non-medical staff as percentages of 
the staff total.

questions.question: What additional percentage of medical staff was present on 31 December 2015 as compared to the annual average full-time medical staff?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must determine the number of medical staff on 
31 December 2015 by subtracting the non-medical 
staff from the staff total. Similarly, we must calcu-
late the annual average of the medical staff in 2015. 
We can then work out the additional percentage of 
medical staff present at year end.
Calculation
We know that:
Medical staff = Staff total – Non medical staff
The number of medical staff present on 31 December 
2015:
= 1 192 852 – 1 018 461
≈ 1 200 000 – 1 020 000
= 180 000
The annual average of medical staff in 2015:
= 868 044 – 713 680
≈ 870 000 – 710 000
= 160 000
The additional percentage of medical staff present 
on 31 December 2015 as compared to the annual 
average full-time medical staff:
= ((180 000 – 160 000) / 160 000) * 100%
= (20 000 / 160 000) * 100%
= (1 / 8) * 100%
= 0.125 * 100%
= 12.5%
Option A has the closest value to our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must take care to read data for 2015 only. No 
other data is required for this question. Option B 
shows the solution based on data for 2020. It is also 
important not to confuse medical staff with non-
medical staff for these calculations. Options C and 
D show the solutions for all hospital staff and non-
medical staff for 2015. Option E gives the ratio for the 
medical staff at year end as compared to the annual 
average. It does not show the additional percentage.

220
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: Which of the following is the best estimate for the annual average number of pupils/ apprentices in 1995?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We can find one estimate by using the number of 
pupils/apprentices at year end as a percentage 
of staff total and the other estimate by using the 
number of pupils/apprentices at year end as a 
percentage of non-medical staff. We can then select 
an option based on these estimates.
Calculation
The number of pupils/apprentices as a percentage of 
staff total at year end of 1995:
= (88 841 / 1 153 200) * 100%
≈ (90 000 / 1 200 000) * 100%
= 7.5%
Estimate for the annual average of pupils/appren-
tices in 1995 based on the above-noted percentage:
= (Annual average of staff total) * (Percentage of pupils 
& apprentices at year end)
= 887 564 * 7.5%
≈ 900 000 * 7.5%
= 67 500
The number of pupils/apprentices as a percentage of 
non-medical staff at year end of 1995:
= (88 841 / 1 036854) * 100%
≈ (90 000 / 1 050 000) * 100%
≈ 8.6%
Estimate for the annual average of pupils/appren-
tices in 1995 based on the above-noted percentage:
= (Annual average of non medical staff) * (Percentage 
of pupils & apprentices at year end)
= 785 974 * 8.6%
≈ 785 000 * 8.6%
≈ 67 500
Option B is clearly by far the closest in value to our 
estimate. Hence, it must be correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You do not need to perform calculations for both 
estimates. The options are quite far apart in value 
and any reasonable calculation will lead to the 
correct option. Approximations and quick calcula-
tions are key to these kinds of questions.
Options D and E give the actual number of pupils/
apprentices at year end and the actual annual 
average of medical staff respectively. Option A is 
just a random number while option C is 10% of the 
annual average of the non-medical staff.

questions.question: On average, how much faster was team USA as compared to team ITA (Italy) for the complete relay race?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the average speeds of each of the 
two teams by using the given distance and times 
and then calculate how much faster was team USA 
relative to team ITA.
Calculation
We know that:
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
The average speed of team USA for the complete 
race (in m/s):
= (400 * 4) / (2 * 60 + 57.3)
= 1 600 / 177.3
≈ 9.024
The average speed of team ITA for the complete race 
(in m/s):
= (400 * 4) / (3 * 60 + 1.2)
= 1 600 / 181.2
≈ 8.830
Percentage by which team USA was faster as 
compared to team ITA (Italy) for the complete relay 
race:
= ((9.024 – 8.830) / 8.830) * 100%
= (0.194 / 8.830) * 100%
≈ 2.20%
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must remember to multiply the distance of each 
lap, i.e., 400 meters, by 4 to account for 4 runners. 
You must also convert the given times to seconds for 
the average speed calculations.

questions.question: At which point was team USA’s average speed the highest?

options.text: At the completion of the race
Team USA maintained the same speed throughout

questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must determine the average speeds of team USA 
at every hand over and at the completion of the race 
to determine at which of the given points of the relay 
race, was team USA’s average speed the highest.

221
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Calculation
We know that:
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
Team USA’s average speed when Hall handed over 
to Norwood (in m/s):
400 / 44.5 ≈ 8.99
Team USA’s average speed when Norwood handed 
over to Robinson (in m/s):
(400 * 2) / (1*60 + 28.5) = 800 / 88.5 ≈ 9.04
Team USA’s average speed when Robinson handed 
over to Benjamin (in m/s):
(400 * 3) / (2*60 + 13.3) = 1 200 / 133.3 ≈ 9.00
Team USA’s average speed at the completion of the 
race (in m/s):
(400 * 4) / (2*60 + 57.3) = 1 600 / 177.3 ≈ 9.02
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must convert all times to seconds at the start of 
the calculations. As can be seen, the average speeds 
only differ in the second decimal place. Hence, you 
must approximate at 2 decimal places.
An alternative method is to calculate all the indi-
vidual times of the 4 runners. Hall’s time was 44.5, 
Norwood’s 44.01, Robinson’s 44.74, and Benjamin’s 
44.02 seconds. With each runner who had a lower 
individual time (Norwood and Benjamin), i.e., a 
faster speed, the average speed of team USA increased, 
while with the addition of each runner having a 
higher time (Hall and Robinson), i.e., a slower speed, 
the average speed of team USA decreased. Hence, 
team USA’s average speed would have been highest 
either at the handover from Norwood to Robinson 
(option B) or at the completion of the race (option D). 
These two options would make good guesses.

questions.question: Assuming team NED (Netherlands) ran at a constant speed throughout, how far behind Robinson (of team USA) was NED’s third runner (in meters) when the handover from Robinson to Benjamin (team USA) was made?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the distance run by team NED 
at the exact time when Robinson handed over to 
Benjamin. We must then subtract this distance from 
the distance run by team USA at that point to find 
the distance between Robinson and team NED’s 
third runner.
Calculation
We know that:
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
Team NED’s average speed for the complete race (in 
m/s):
= (400 * 4) / (3*60 + 0.4)
≈ 1 600 / 180
≈ 8.87
By re-arranging the average speed formula, we get:
Total distance = Average speed * Total time
Distance run (in meters) by team NED when 
Robinson handed over to Benjamin, i.e., after 02:13.3:
= 8.87 * (2*60 + 13.3)
= 8.87 * (120 + 13.3)
= 8.87 * 133.3
≈ 1 182
The distance between Robinson and team NED’s 
third runner when Robinson handed over to 
Benjamin, i.e., at the 400 * 3 = 1 200m mark:
= 1 200 – 1 182 
= 18
Option D is clearly the closest in value to our 
estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Rounding the numbers helped speed up the calcu-
lation. However, the margins in this question are 
minimal so approximating too much might lead you 
to a different option.

questions.question: In which of the following years were most terrorism suspects arrested per fatality caused in terrorist attacks in the EU?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the number of terrorism suspects 
arrested per fatality caused in terrorist attacks in 
the EU for each of the five given years. We can then 
identify the year having the highest value.
Calculation
Number of terrorism suspects arrested per fatality 
caused in terrorist attacks in the EU each year:
In 2010: = 611 / 7 ≈ 609 / 7 = 87
In 2013: = 631 / 7 ≈ 90

222
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
In 2014: = 774/ 4 ≈ 780 / 4 = 195 
In 2016: = 1 011 / 142 ≈ 1 015 / 145 = 7
In 2019: = 1 316 / 10 = 131.6
2014 had the highest the highest number of terrorism 
suspects arrested per fatality caused in terrorist 
attacks in the EU.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is very important to use the data of the given years 
only. It would be a waste of time to calculate the 
value for any other year.
Think about this question in terms of fractions. A frac-
tion with a larger numerator and a smaller denomi-
nator yields a greater value. For 2010, the value is 
approximately 87. The numerator in 2013 is larger 
and the denominator is the same. Hence, the value 
for 2013 is automatically larger. The fraction for 2014 
has a bigger numerator and a smaller denominator 
and the value is around 200. This means that 2014 
has a higher value than that of 2013. The denomi-
nator for 2016 is quite a big number which implies 
that the result is a small number. The value for 2019 is 
fairly obvious. Therefore, after mentally comparing, 
and not actually calculating, the five fractions we can 
conclude that 2014 has the highest value.

questions.question: What percentage of the terrorism suspects arrested in 2015 had a known affiliation?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the number of suspects arrested 
in 2015 who had known affiliations. We must then 
convert that number into a percentage.
Calculation
Suspects listed in the ‘Not specified’ column are 
not affiliated with any of the first four categories. 
Hence, if we want to quickly ascertain the number 
of suspects with known affiliations, we can simply 
subtract the ‘Not specified’ number from the total 
number of suspects.
The number of suspects arrested in 2015 who had 
known affiliations:
= 1 077 – 144 
= 933
Percentage of suspects arrested in 2015 who had 
known affiliations:
= (933 / 1 077) * 100%
≈ 86.6%
Option E is clearly the correct answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
This is a simple enough calculation provided you 
use the right data from the table. Otherwise, you 
might end up with a result like option D, which is the 
percentage for 2014. Option A gives the percentage of 
suspects with the affiliation ‘Not specified’. Option 
B gives the percentage of Jihadist suspects while 
option C is the sum of the percentages of Jihadist 
and Separatist suspects. The fatalities data is irrel-
evant in this question.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of affiliations accounted for more than 60% of terrorism- related arrests in each year from 2016 to 2018 in the EU?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must look at the suspects data for the given years 
and analyze which affiliations carry the bulk of the 
weight. We must then work out which of the given 
pairs of affiliations accounted for more than 60% of 
terrorism-related arrests in each year from 2016 to 
2018 in the EU.
Calculation
Looking at the data, we can see that the ‘Jihadist’ 
affiliation had by far the most arrested suspects in 
each year from 2016 to 2018. In fact, the ‘Jihadist’ 
affiliation accounted for more than 50% of suspects 
in 2016 and 2017 and nearly 50% of suspects in 2018. 
Hence, ‘Jihadist’ must be one of the affiliations we 
are looking for.
The suspects with ‘Not specified’ affiliations 
accounted for the second-highest number of suspects 
in each of the three years. 
Therefore, it would be impossible to have 60% of 
the suspects in each year from 2016 to 2018 without 
both the Jihadist’ and the ‘Not specified’ affiliations, 
making A the only possible answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The risk with this question is that you end up doing 
a lot of unnecessary calculations when all that is 
needed is to think about what the data is telling you. 

223
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: In 2022, 721 000 females in Portugal were of the age 75+. How many males in Portugal were in the same age group in 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must construct an equation such that the total 
number of 75+ people is equal to the sum of the 
number of 75+ males and 75+ females to calculate 
the total population of Portugal in 2022. We can 
then use the percentage of 75+ males to calculate the 
number of 75+ males.
Calculation
Let M, be the number of 75+ males in Portugal in 
2022.
Let P, be the total population of Portugal in 2022.
4.6% of the total population were males of age 75+ in 
Portugal in 2022.
11.6% of the total population were of age 75+ in 
Portugal in 2022.
We know that:
(Total no. of people aged 75+) = (No. of males of age 
75+) + (No. of females of age 75+)
(11.6% * P) = (4.6% * P) + (721 000)
0.116P = 0.046P + 721 000
0.116P – 0.046P = 721 000
0.07P = 721 000
P = (721 000 / 0.07)
= 72 100 000 / 7
= 10 300 000
Calculate the number of 75+ males in Portugal in 
2022:
M = 4.6% * P
M = 4.6% * 10 300 000
M = (4.6 / 100) * 10 300 000
M = 4.6 * 103 000
M = (4 * 103 000) + 0.6 * 103 000
M = 412 000 + 61 800 = 473 800
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
If you’ve no time for proper calculations, you could 
try some very crude rounding that you can do in your 
head. We know from the table that 11.6% of the popu-
lation are 75+ and that 4.6% of males are 75+. Round 
the percentages to 12 and 5, so you now have 7 (i.e., 12 
– 5) women for every 5 men. There are 721 000 women 
who are 75+, which you can simplify to 700 000. You 
then simply divide by 7 and multiply by 5 to get an 
estimate of 500 000 men. The nearest answer option 
is A, which is the right answer. Such crude rounding 
won’t always work, but it is a worthwhile fallback 
option if the alternative is just wildly guessing.

questions.question: In 2022, 430 000 people in Ireland were in the age group 65-74. What was Ireland’s tota population in 2022?

options.text: 5 058 824 on of the respective country. al

questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must find a percentage for the age group 65-74 
in Ireland in 2022. We must then construct an equa-
tion to use the given number and the calculated 
percentage of people in the 65-74 age group to calcu-
late the total population of Ireland in 2022.
Calculation
Let I, be the total population of Ireland in 2022.
The age group 65+ includes not only those who are 
65-74 but also those in the age group 75+. Hence:
(People in age group 65+) = (People in age group 
65-74) + (People in age group 75+)
Therefore:
14.8% = (People in age group 65-74) + 6.3%
(People in age group 65-74) = 14.8% – 6.3% = 8.5%
Calculate the total population of Ireland in 2022:
I * 8.5% = 430 000
I = 430 000 / 8.5%
I = 430 000 / (8.5 / 100)
I = 430 000 * (100 / 8.5)
I = 430 000 000 / 85
I ≈ 5 060 000
Option B is closest in value to our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is crucial to understand that the 75+ age group 
is a subset of the 65+ age group. This is confirmed 
by the fact that when the percentages of all age 
groups up to the 65+ age group are added together 
they account for 100% of the total population. If the 
percentage for the 65+ age group is used instead of 
the 65-74 age group, our solution will be equal to 
option A. Similarly, if only the percentage of the 75+ 
age group were to be used in the calculations, the 
solution would equal option C.
Option D is the total population of Portugal in 
2022 from the previous question. The value given 
in option E can be arrived at when the percentages 
of males are used instead of the percentages of all 
people in respective age groups.

224
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: Given that Portugal had 83% more people than Ireland who were less than 65 years old, what was the ratio of the number of people in Ireland relative to Portugal who were less than 18 years of age?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the given facts to construct an equation such 
that the ratio of the number people in Ireland rela-
tive to Portugal who were less than 18 years of age 
can be computed.
Calculation
Let E, be the number of people who were less than 18 
years of age in Ireland in 2022.
Let T, be the number of people who were less than 18 
years of age in Portugal in 2022.
Let I, be the total population of Ireland in 2022.
Let P, be the total population of Portugal in 2022.
We need to evaluate the ratio, E / T.
We know that:
E = 24.3% * I = 0.243I
I = E / 0.243
and 
T = 15.8% * P = 0.158P
P = T / 0.158
The total percentage of people in Ireland in 2022 who 
were less than 65 years of age:
= (Total population) – (Percentage of people in age 
group 65+)
= 100% – 14.8% = 85.2%
The total percentage of people in Portugal in 2022 
who were less than 65 years of age:
= (Total population) – (Percentage of people in age 
group 65+)
= 100% – 23.7% = 76.3%
We are told that Portugal had 83% more people than 
Ireland who were less than 65 years old:
((No. of people in Portugal less than 65 years old) / (No. 
of people in Ireland less than 65 years old)) * 100% = 
183%
((76.3% * P) / (85.2% * I)) * 100% = 183%
(76.3% * P) / (85.2% * I) = 1.83
0.763P / 0.852I = 1.83
P / I = 1.83 * (0.852 / 0.763)
P / I ≈ 2.04
Now we can substitute the expressions for P and I:
(T / 0.158) / (E / 0.243) = 2.04
T / 0.158 = 2.04 * (E / 0.243)
E / T = 0.243 / (0.158 * 2.04)
E / T ≈ 0.75
Option B is the closest to our approximation and the 
answer to choose.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is very important to construct the equations care-
fully because a slight error can be very costly. Option 
D is the reciprocal of the correct value. Option E is 
the ratio of the total populations of Portugal and 
Ireland.
There were a lot of calculations in this answer, but 
there is a much shorter route if we go back to the first 
two questions in this set. For the first question we 
worked out that that the total population of Portugal 
was 10 300 000. For the second question we calcu-
lated that the total population of Ireland was 5 060 
000. This means that Ireland’s total population is 
almost half that of Portugal. Therefore, 16% (people 
of less than 18 years of age) of Portugal’s population 
would equal approximately 2 * 16 = 32% of Ireland’s 
population. Hence, the ratio would be 24% / 32% = 
0.75.

questions.question: Given that the total population of France was 67.9 million in 2022, what was its national debt that year (in trillions of U.S. Dollars)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use France’s GDP per capita and total 
population for 2022 to calculate its GDP. We must 
then use the national debt to GDP ratio to calculate 
France’s national debt in 2022.
Calculation
We know that:
GDP per capita = GDP / Total population
Hence:
GDP = GDP per capita * Total population
GDP of France in 2022 (in trillions of U.S. dollars):
= (42 409 * 67.9) / 1 000 000
≈ (42 500 * 68) / 1 000 000
= 2.89

225
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
We also know that:
National debt to GDP ratio = National debt / GDP
France’s national debt to GDP ratio in 2022 was 
111.1%.
Hence, France’s national debt in 2022 was (in tril-
lions of U.S. dollars):
= 2.89 * 111.1%
= 2.89 * (111.1 / 100)
≈ 3.21
Option C is the closest in value to our estimation. 
Hence, it is probably correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must be careful to read the data for France 
only as there are 8 countries given and the bars are 
aligned differently in the charts. It is also crucial to 
deal with the different orders of magnitude prop-
erly. The population has been provided in millions 
(6 zeroes) whereas the national debt is required 
in trillions (12 zeroes). Option B gives the GDP of 
France in 2022. Option A divides the GDP, instead of 
multiplying it, by 111.1%.

questions.question: The forecast GDP growth rate of advanced economies, including United States, was 0.5% for 2023 and 1.6% for 2024. Assuming no changes in the total population and in the national debt to GDP ratio, what was the forecast national debt per capita of the United States for 2024 (in U.S. Dollars)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first find an estimate for the GDP per capita 
of the United States by using the forecast growth 
rates for 2023 and 2024. We can then use the national 
debt to GQP ratio of 2022 to find an estimate for the 
national debt of the United States in 2024.
Calculation
The GDP per capita for the United States in 2022 was 
U.S. dollars 76 348.
The estimated United States GDP per capita (in U.S. 
dollars) for 2023 was:
= 76 348 * (100% + 0.5%)
= 76 348 * 1.005
≈ 76 730
The estimated United States GDP per capita (in U.S. 
dollars) for 2024 was:
= 76 730 * (100% + 1.6%)
= 76 730 * 1.016
≈ 77 958
The national debt to GDP ratio of the United States 
in 2022 was 121.7%.
The estimated United States national debt per capita 
(in U.S. dollars) for 2024 was:
= 77 958 * 121.7%
= 77 958 * 1.217
≈ 94 875
Option D is the same as our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must compound the GDP growth separately. 
You cannot add the two growth rate percentages 
together and apply them in a single calculation. If 
this mistake is made then the solution will equal the 
incorrect option E.
Since we used GDP per capita in the national debt 
to GDP ratio, the solution will automatically be 
national debt per capita.

questions.question: Given that Portugal had 83% more people than Ireland who were less than 65 years old, what was the ratio of the number of people in Ireland relative to Portugal who were less than 18 years of age?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the given average annual forecast GDP 
growth rate for the United Kingdom until 2027 to 
estimate United Kingdom’s GDP per capita in 2027. 
We must then use this estimate with the given 
national debt to GDP ratio for 2027 to calculate the 
national debt per capita for that year. 
Calculation
United Kingdom’s estimated GDP per capita in 2027 
(in U.S. dollars) is:
45 295 * (100% + 2%)⁵
= 45 295 * (102%)⁵
= 45 295 * (1.02)⁵
≈ 45 295 * 1.104
≈ 50 000
United Kingdom’s estimated national debt per 
capita in 2027 (in U.S. dollars) is:
= 50 000 * 113%
= 50 000 * 1.13
= 56 500
Our estimate is closest to option D. 

226
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The annual growth rates of the GDP must be 
compounded for 5 years, i.e., from 2022 to 2027 to be 
accurate. You must not use the national debt to GDP 
ratio for 2022, because the ratio for 2027 has already 
been provided to us (113%).
Although the answer options A, B, C and D are fairly 
close together, which would usually discourage 
more ambitious estimating, in this case it would 
prove effective. Round the 2022 GDP per capita to 
45 000. Then, rather than compound the 2% growth, 
simply multiply 2% by 5 to get 10% growth by 2027, 
which gives 49 500. Then multiply this by 1.13 to get 
55 935. This also gives you the correct answer D and 
you would only need to use a calculator for the final 
step.

questions.question: How much more cow milk fat content was produced in Spain than in Italy in 2018 (in tonnes)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the respective fat content percentages 
along with the cow milk production to find the cow 
milk fat content produced in Spain and Italy. We can 
then find the difference between the two.
Calculation
Total cow milk fat content produced in Italy in 2018 
(in tonnes):
= (2 470 * 1 000) * 3.71%
≈ (2 500 * 1 000) * 3.7%
≈ 93 000
Total cow milk fat content produced in Spain in 2018 
(in tonnes):
= (3 257 * 1 000) * 3.68%
≈ (3 250 * 1 000) * 3.7%
≈ 120 250
The difference in the total cow milk fat content 
produced in Spain and Italy in 2018 (in tonnes):
= 120 250 – 93 000
= 27 250
Option A is clearly the closest value to our estimate. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Reading the data for the incorrect year may result 
in solutions equal to option B or C. Although our 
estimated result was a bit off the correct answer, the 
widely separated option values made that irrelevant.
To make the calculation slightly quicker, we could 
have assumed the same cow milk fat content 
percentage for Italy and Spain (which were already 
very close) and subtracted the cow milk produc-
tion first and then multiplied it by the fat content 
percentage.

questions.question: How much more protein content was produced in 2022 in Finland due to a higher protein content percentage as compared to the protein content that would have been produced in 2022 had the protein content percentage stayed at the level of 2018 (in tonnes)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must determine the protein content produced in 
Finland in 2022 as well as the protein content that 
would have been produced in 2022 had the protein 
content percentage stayed at the level of 2018. Then, 
we can find the difference between the two.
Calculation
Cow milk protein content produced in Finland in 
2022 (in thousand tonnes): 
= 533 * 4.34%
≈ 23.13
Cow milk protein content that would have been 
produced in 2022 had the protein content percentage 
stayed at the level of 2018 (in thousand tonnes): 
= 533 * 4.44%
≈ 23.67
Difference in protein content had the protein content 
percentage not improved (in tonnes): 
= (23.67 – 23.13) * 1 000
= 0.54 * 1 000
= 540
Option B is the closest in value. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must take care to read the data for the correct 
year and country as we have switched both year and 
country from the previous question. Option E is the 
required protein content difference, but for Italy.
You do not need to use the Finland’s cow milk 
production data for 2018 since your calculations 
must be based on the production level of 2022. The 
only difference to consider is that of the protein 
content percentages. Had we used the different 
production levels for 2018 and 2022, our solution 
would have equaled option D. Similarly, had we 
used the production level of 2018 only, our solution 
would have been equal to option C.

227
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
You must also be careful of the change in the order of 
magnitude. The solution is required in tonnes. If you 
forget to change it, your solution will equal option A.

questions.question: What was the percentage change in the combined yield of cow milk fat and protein content in Lithuania from 2020 to 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must separately calculate the combined cow 
milk fat and protein content produced in Lithuania 
in 2020 and 2022. We must then calculate the 
percentage change between those two values.
Calculation
The combined cow milk fat and protein content 
percentage for Lithuania in 2020:
= 4.17% + 3.38%
= 7.55%
The combined cow milk fat and protein content yield 
for Lithuania in 2020 (in thousand tonnes):
= 86 * 7.55%
≈ 6.49
The combined cow milk fat and protein content 
percentage for Lithuania in 2022:
= 4.27% + 3.44%
= 7.71%
The combined cow milk fat and protein content yield 
for Lithuania in 2022 (in thousand tonnes):
= 69 * 7.71%
≈ 5.32
The percentage change in the combined yield of cow 
milk fat and protein content in Lithuania from 2020 
to 2022:
= ((5.32 – 6.49) / 6.49) * 100%
= (–1.17 / 6.49) * 100%
≈ –18.2% 
Option C is the closest in value to our approximate 
solution. Hence, it is probably correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is very important to read the data for the correct 
years and the correct country. Had the data of 2018 
and 2022 been used, option B would have matched 
our solution. Option A is simply the percentage 
change in the cow milk production of Lithuania 
from 2020 to 2022. Similarly, option D is the 
percentage change in the combined fat and protein 
content percentages from 2020 to 2022.

questions.question: What was the change in Euros per tonne of the landings of fishery products (for industrial use) in Iceland from 2017 to 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the value and tonnage of landings of 
fishery products (for industrial use) in 2017 and 2021 
to calculate the ratio and then subtract the ratios to 
find the difference.
Calculation
Euros per tonne of the landings of fishery products 
(for industrial use) in Iceland in 2017:
= (71 591 * 1 000) / 500 944
≈ (72 000 * 1 000) / 500 000
= 720 / 5
= 144
And for 2021:
= (65 423 * 1 000) / 330 856
≈ (65 000 * 1 000) / 330 000
= 6 500 / 33
≈ 197
The change in euros per tonne of the landings of 
fishery products (for industrial use) in Iceland from 
2017 to 2021:
= 197 – 144 
= +53
Based on our approximate calculation, option C is 
clearly the correct answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is a must to read the data for the correct year and 
country. If you compare the ratios for 2019 and 2021, 
your solution will match option A. And if you calcu-
late the ratio for Norway, instead of Iceland, your 
solution would be equal to option D.
It is important to note that the value of the landings 
of fishery products (for industrial use) is in thou-
sands and you must account for this in your calcula-
tions. If the value is not converted, you might get an 
incorrect solution equal to option B. 
Option E provides the ratio for Iceland in 2021 only.

228
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: How much more protein content was produced in 2022 in Finland due to a higher protein content percentage as compared to the protein content that would have been produced in 2022 had the protein content percentage stayed at the level of 2018 (in tonnes)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must evaluate the ratio, euros per tonne, of land-
ings of fishery products (for human consumption) in 
Spain in 2019. We must then use the given difference 
to determine the ratio for landings of fishery prod-
ucts (for industrial use). Finally, we can use this ratio 
to determine the tonnage of the landings of fishery 
products (for industrial use) for Spain in 2019.
Calculation
Euros per tonne of the landings of fishery products 
(for human consumption) in Spain in 2019:
= (1 855.6 * 1 000 000) / 781 610
≈ (1 850 * 1 000 000) / 780 000
= 185 000 / 78
≈ 2 370
Euros per tonne of the landings of fishery products 
(for industrial use) in Spain in 2019:
= (Ratio for human consumption) – (Given difference)
= 2 370 – 1 761.8
≈ 610
We know that:
Euros per tonne = Euros / Tonnes
Hence:
Tonnes = Euros / Euros per tonne
The tonnage of the landings of fishery products (for 
industrial use) for Spain in 2019:
= (1 941 * 1 000) / 610
≈ (1 950 * 1 000) / 600
= 3 250
Option C is the closest to our estimated value.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is vital to account for the different orders of 
magnitude for euro values provided for human 
consumption (in millions) and for industrial use (in 
thousands). Options A and E reflect such possible 
errors in solutions. 
The magnitude of tonnage values in options D and 
E are too high. They do not correspond to the small 
euro value of Spain (for Industrial use) in 2019. 
Hence, they can be eliminated easily.

questions.question: What was the percentage change in the combined yield of cow milk fat and protein content in Lithuania from 2020 to 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the change in euros per tonne 
of the landings of fishery products (for human 
consumption) in France from 2017 to 2021 to estimate

change to project the euros per tonne value for 2026.
Calculation
Change in euros per tonne value of the landings of 
fishery products (for human consumption) in France 
from 2017 to 2021:
= ((819.0 * 1 000 000) / 283 671) – ((862.2 * 1 000 000) / 
304 085)
≈ ((820 * 1 000 000) / 285 000) – ((860 * 1 000 000) / 305 
000)
≈ 2 880 – 2 820
= 60
Annual change in euros per tonne value of the land-
ings of fishery products (for human consumption) in 
France from 2017 to 2021:
= 60 / 4
= 15
Projected value of euros per tonne of the landings of 
fishery products (for human consumption) in France 
in 2026:
= (Euros per tonne in 2021) + (Annual change) * 
(Number of projected years)
= 2 880 + 15 * 5
= 2 880 + 75
= 2 955
Option E is closest in value to our estimate and hence 
is our best choice.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Option D provides the projected value for 2023, if 
you calculate that by mistake. 
Option C gives the projected value for 2026 of 
Iceland. Similarly, you must only use data from the 
table ‘for human consumption’. If you mistakenly 
use the table ‘for industrial use’, your solution will

euro values in millions. You must convert the order
