numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: Which countries had the highest and the lowest electricity consumptions per capita i 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must evaluate the per capita ratios for all six 
countries mentioned in the options in order to deter-
mine which countries have the highest and lowest 
values.
Calculation
We know that:
Electricity consumption per capita in 2022, in kW h 
per capita:
= (Electricity consumption per capita in 2022, in kW h) / 
(Total population) 
= ((Electricity consumption per capita in 2022, in kW h) 
/ (1 000 000) / (Total population / 1 000 000))
= (Electricity consumption per capita in 2022, in GW h) 
/ (Total population, in millions)
Electricity consumption per capita in 2022 (in kW-h 
per capita):
Iceland: 20 108 / 0.4 ≈ 20 100 / 0.4 = 50 250
Nigeria: 32 126 / 218.5 ≈ 32 500 / 220 ≈ 150
Norway: 152 677 / 5.4 ≈ 152 500 / 5 = 30 500
India: 1 838 074 / 1 417.2 ≈ 1 850 000 / 1 400 ≈ 1 320
Kuwait: 82 957 / 4.3 ≈ 83 000 / 4 = 20 750
China: 8 839 076 / 1 425.9 ≈ 8 850 000 / 1 425 ≈ 6 200
Therefore, Iceland had the highest electricity 
consumption per capita in 2022 while Nigeria had 
the lowest.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is important to only read the data for the six coun-
tries mentioned in the options. Calculating the per 
capita figures for any other country would be a 
waste of valuable time.
As mentioned in the formula, calculating the ratios 
with the given units yields the correct result since 
both the numerator and denominator represent the 
order of magnitude of a million. Even if the order 
of magnitudes had been different, simply dividing 
the two numbers would have sufficed since we only 
needed to compare the ratios of the countries and 
not provide exact answers.

questions.question: What was the difference in the electricity consumption per capita in the United States and Germany in 2022 (in kW-h)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must separately calculate the electricity 
consumption per capita in the United States and 
Germany in 2022 and then find the difference.
Calculation
We know that:
Electricity consumption per capita in 2022, in kW h 
per capita:
= (Electricity consumption per capita in 2022, in GW h) 
/ (Total population in millions)
Electricity consumption per capita (in kW-h) in the 
United States in 2022:
= 4 296 958 / 338.3
≈ 4 300 000 / 340
≈ 12 650
Electricity consumption per capita (in kW-h) in 
Germany in 2022:
= 582 255 / 83.4
≈ 585 000 / 85
≈ 6 880
The difference in the electricity consumption per 
capita in the United States and Germany in 2022 (in 
kW-h):
= 12 650 – 6 880
= 5 770
Based on our estimate, option B is the correct answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is important to find the country per capita figures 
before finding the difference. It would be incorrect to 
find the differences of the total electricity consump-
tions and total populations first and then calculate 
the ratio based on these differences. Option E would 
match the solution of such an incorrect calcula-
tion. Options C and D provide the actual electricity 
consumption per capita in Germany and the United 
States respectively. 

230
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: Based on the Euros per tonne values of the landings of fishery products (for human consumption) in France in 2017 and in 2021, what is France’s projected Euros per tonne value for 2026?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first calculate the projected electricity 
consumption and the total population of China in 
2023 based on the provided growth rates. Then, we 
must calculate the electricity consumption per capita 
for 2023 and eventually the increase from 2022.
Calculation
The projected electricity consumption of China in 
2023 (in GW-h):
= 8 839 076 * (100% + 5%)
≈ 8 840 000 * 1.05
= 9 282 000
The projected total population of China in 2023 (in 
millions):
= 1 425.9 * (100% – 0.5%)
≈ 1 425 * 0.995
≈ 1 418
The projected electricity consumption of China in 
2023 (in GW-h):
= 8 839 076 * (100% + 5%)
≈ 8 840 000 * 1.05
= 9 282 000
The projected electricity consumption per capita of 
China in 2023 (in kW-h):
= 9 282 000 / 1 418
≈ 6 545
The electricity consumption per capita of China in 
2022 (in kW-h):
= 8 839 076 / 1 425.9 
≈ 8 840 000 / 1 425 
≈ 6 200
The projected increase in electricity consumption per 
capita of China from 2022 to 2023 (in kW-h):
= 6 545 – 6 200 
= 345
Option D is the correct answer based on our 
estimation.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Option A can only be correct if both the electricity 
consumption and the total population are projected 
to grow at the same rate. That is not the case. 
Option B’s value is obtained by decreasing 2022’s 
per capita figure by 5% – 0.5% = 4.5%. This is obvi-
ously absurd logic. Option E provides the projected 
per capita figure for 2023. This is not what has been 
asked for.
It is important to find the country per capita figures 
before finding the difference. It would be incorrect to 
find the differences of the total electricity consump-
tions and total populations first and then calculate 
the ratio based on these differences. Option E would 
match the solution of such an incorrect calculation.

questions.question: What was the approximate number of overnight stays made in holiday and other short-stay accommodation by tourists from Austria in Germany in 2022 (in millions)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must multiply the percentage of overnight stays 
in holiday and other short-stay accommodations in 
Germany in 2022 with the approximate number of 
tourists from Austria who visited Germany in 2022.
Calculation
The percentage of overnight stays in holiday and 
other short-stay accommodation in Germany in 2022 
was 21.8%.
The approximate number of tourists from Austria 
who visited Germany in 2022, from the bar chart, 
was 3.5 million.
The approximate number of overnight stays made 
in holiday and other short-stay accommodation 
by tourists from Austria in Germany in 2022 (in 
millions):
= 3.5 * 21.8%
≈ 3.5 * 22%
= 3.5 * (10% + 10% + 1% + 1%)
= 3.5 * 10% + 3.5 * 10% + 3.5 * 1% + 3.5 * 1%
= 0.35 + 0.35 + 0.035 + 0.035
= 0.77
As per our approximate calculation, option B is 
clearly by far the closest value. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The bars in the bar chart are not labelled. Hence, we 
will have to read approximate values. This might cause 

231
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
some variation in our solution. In our calculation, 
when multiplying the percentage with the number 
of tourists, we broke the problem down into smaller 
easier and quickly solvable bits. Finding 1% and 10% 
of numbers is always easier. This process might seem 
longer, but it is more efficient and saves time.

questions.question: Given that there were 50 million overnight stays by non-citizens in Germany in 2022, approximately what percentage of them were not made in camping grounds by non- European tourists (limited to the given data)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the percentage of overnights stays 
not made in camping grounds. We must also ascer-
tain the total number of overnight stays made by 
non-Europeans (according to the bar chart). Then, 
we must multiply these two to find our solution.
Calculation
The percentage of overnight stays in camping 
grounds in Germany in 2022 was 12.5%. Hence, the 
percentage of overnight stays not made in camping 
grounds in Germany in 2022:
= 100% – 12.5% 
= 87.5%
There are only citizens of two non-European coun-
tries: United States and Arabs. 
The total number of overnight stays (in millions, 
from the bar chart) made by the citizens of the United 
States and the Arabs:
= 5.4 + 1.2
= 6.6
The number of overnight stays made by non-
European citizens in accommodations other than 
camping grounds in Germany in 2022:
= 6.6 * 87.5% 
≈ 6.6 * 88%
= 6.6 * (100% – 10% – 1% – 1%)
= 6.6 – 6.6 * 10% – 6.6 * 1% – 6.6 * 1%
= 6.6 – 0.66 – 0.066 - 0.066
= 5.94 – 0.132
≈ 5.8
The percentage of overnight stays made by non-
European citizens in accommodations other than 
camping grounds in Germany in 2022 was:
= (5.8 / 50) * 100%
= (1.16 / 10) * 100% 
= 11.6%
Option C is the closest in value. Hence, it is probably 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must use the gridlines on the bar chart to make 
as accurate an approximation as possible.
The great majority of the tourists to Germany were 
clearly Europeans, so option E is out of the ques-
tion. One-eighth of overnight stays were in camping 
grounds. Therefore, seven-eighths of the overnight 
stays must have been in other accommodations. This 
means that very small percentages, like option A, are 
unlikely.
Option B accounts for citizens of the United States 
only and ignores the Arabs. Option D on the other 
hand includes all non-Europeans and all accommo-
dations, including the camping grounds.

questions.question: There were 150% more overnight stays in Germany in January 2022 as compared to 12 months earlier. Given that there were 16.2 million overnight stays in January 2022 and that the percentages of overnight stays in different accommodations were mostly unchanged since 2021, approximately how many overnight stays (in millions) were in hotels, inns and guesthouses in January 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
First, we must determine the number of over-
night stays in January 2021 by using the number 
of overnight stays in January 2022 and the growth 
percentage. We must then use the percentage of 
overnights stays in hotels, inns and guesthouses to 
find the overnight stays (in millions) in hotels, inns 
and guesthouses in January 2021.
Calculation
Number of overnight stays in January 2022:
= (No. of overnight stays in January 2021) * (100% + 
150%)
Hence:
Number of overnight stays in January 2021:
= (Number of overnight stays in January 2022) / 250%
The number of overnight stays (in millions) in 
January 2021:
= 16.2 / 250%
= 16.2 / 2.5
≈ (15 + 1.25) / 2.5
= (15 / 2.5) + (1.25 / 2.5)
= 6 + 0.5
= 6.5
The percentage of overnights stays in hotels, inns 
and guesthouses in 2022 was 56.5% which was 

232
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
unchanged from 2021. 
Overnight stays (in millions) in hotels, inns and 
guesthouses in January 2021:
= 6.5 * 56.5%
≈ 3.67
Option A is likely to be correct based on our approxi-
mate solution.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
No information from the bar chart is required in 
this question since the given number of overnight 
stays is not limited to foreign tourists.
It is quite easy to confuse a 150% increase with a 
50% increase. 150% increase means that the number 
increased by 1.5 times the original number. If the 
increase was taken to mean 50% only, the solution 
would match option B for hotels, inns and guesthouses 
only, or option E if all accommodations were consid-
ered. Option D gives the number of overnight stays in 
hotels, inns and guesthouses in January 2022. Option C 
is the total number of overnight stays in January 2021.
After reading the question we knew that we were 
looking for a solution that was nearly half of a 
number which was 2.5 times smaller than 16.2 
million. Option A is probably the only solution in 
that range.

questions.question: What was the average speed of Carlos Sainz car for the complete race (in km/h)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the race completion time for 
Carlos Sainz and then use the total distance for 
which his car was driven to evaluate his average 
speed for the race.
Calculation
Sainz’s race completion time (in hours):
= (Winner’s time) + (Sainz’s additional time)
= 1:33:56.7 + 48.052
= 1 + (33 / 60) + ((56.7 + 48.052) / (60 * 60))
= 1 + 0.55 + (104.752 / 3 600)
≈ 1.55 + 0.029
= 1.579
Total distance driven by Sainz (in km):
= (Number of laps) * (Lap length)
= 57 * 5.412
≈ 308.5
Average speed = Total distance/ Total time 
The average speed of Carlos Sainz’s car for the 
complete race (in km/h):
= 308.5 / 1.579
≈ 309 / 1.58
≈ 195.6
Option C is close enough to our approximate solu-
tion to be correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The margin of victory in the race is measured in 
seconds, so the average speeds of the cars are very 
close together. Options B, C and D all present a real-
istic set of speeds for such a race so in this case you 
have to do the calculations. In fact, option B is the 
average speed of the contestant in 10th place while 
option D gives the average speed of the winner.

questions.question: Assuming that the combined average speed of 2 cars is the ratio of the sum of the distan driven by both cars and the sum of the time taken by both cars, how much slower (in km/h) were the 2 Aston Martin Aramco Mercedes cars than the 2 Red Bull Racing Honda RBPT cars in the race? km z’s ce
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must determine the total times taken by each 
pair of cars. We must then find the average speed of 
each pair and find the difference.
Calculation
Total time taken by the 2 Aston Martin Aramco 
Mercedes cars (in hours):
= 2 * (Winner’s time) + (Alonso’s additional time) + 
(Stroll’s additional time)
= 2 * (1:33:56.7) + ((38.637 + 54.502) / 3 600)
= 2 * (1 + (33 / 60) + (56.7 / 3 600)) + (93.139 / 3 600)
≈ 2 * (1.566) + 0.026
= 3.132 + 0.029
= 3.16
Total time taken by the 2 Red Bull Racing Honda 
RBPT cars (in hours):
= 2 * (Winner’s time) + (Perez’s additional time)
= 2 * (1:33:56.7) + (11.987 / 3 600)
≈ 2 * (1.566) + 0.003
= 3.132 + 0.003
= 3.135
Combined average speed:
= Total distance covered by both cars / Total time taken 
by both cars
Combined average speed of the 2 Aston Martin 

233
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Aramco Mercedes cars (in km/h):
= (308.5 * 2) / 3.16
= 617 / 3.16
≈ 195.25
Combined average speed of the 2 Red Bull Racing 
Honda RBPT cars (in km/h):
= 617 / 3.135
≈ 196.81
Difference between the combined average speeds (in 
km/h):
= 196.81 – 195.25
= 1.56
Option B is the closest in value to our approximate 
solution.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The margins are quite slim so you can approximate 
only slightly. Options A and C give the difference 
in the average speeds of Verstappen with Perez and 
Bottas respectively. Option D is equal to twice the 
difference in combined speeds of the 2 pairs of cars. 
Given that the average difference in speeds is very 
small, around 1.5 km/h, we should expect nothing 
extraordinary from the difference in combined 
average speeds. Option B is the obvious candidate 
for a guess.

questions.question: George Russell’s fastest lap time was 1 minute and 37.22 seconds. What would have been his position had he constantly driven at 98% of that average speed throughout the race?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must multiply Russell’s fastest lap time by the 
number of laps. Then we must calculate his average 
speed for the race and multiply it by 98%. Finally, we 
need to calculate his revised race completion time 
with the new average speed to determine his posi-
tion in the race.
Calculation
Race time (in hours) based on George Russell’s 
fastest lap time:
= (57 * (1 * 60 + 37.22)) / 3 600
= (57 * 97.22) / 3 600
≈ 5 542 / 3 600
≈ 1.539
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
George Russell’s average speed for the complete race 
based on the fastest lap time (in km/h): = 308.5 / 
1.539 ≈ 200.45
George Russell’s average speed for the complete race 
based on 98% of the fastest lap time (in km/h):
= 200.45 * 98%
≈ 196.45
Total time = Total distance / Average speed
Total time taken by George Russell to complete race 
at 98% of the average speed of his fastest lap time:
= 308.5 / 196.45 = 1.570 hours
= 1 hour + 0.57 * 60 minutes
= 1 hour + 34 minutes + 0.2 * 60 seconds
= 1 hour + 34 minutes + 12 seconds = 1:34:12
Additional time required by George Russell from 
winner’s time:
= 1:34:12 – 1:33:56.7 = 15.3 seconds
George Russell would have finished third had he 
driven at 98% of the average speed of his fastest lap 
throughout the race.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
There quite a few consecutive calculations to do. 
Hence, it is better not to make too many approxima-
tions, especially in the earlier steps. As it turns out, 
our calculated time was comfortably in the big gap 
between the second and third placed contestants. 
An alternative method could have been to divide 
the race time based on the fastest lap time by 98%. 
This would have given us the revised race time for 
Russell more quickly.

questions.question: In 2021, what was the highest percentage contribution by any product to the respective country’s total trade services?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must identify products with the highest turn-
over share for the respective countries and find their 
corresponding percentage contributions to the total 
trade services rendered.
Calculation
Computer programming, consultancy and related 
activities had the highest share of turnover in both 
France and Sweden in 2021.

234
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Share in France:
= (65 804 / 173 320) * 100%
≈ (66 000 / 174 000) * 100%
= ((22 * 3 000) / (58 * 3 000)) * 100%
= (22 / 58) * 100%
≈ 38%
Share in Sweden:
= (36 833 / 74 175) * 100%
≈ (37 000 / 74 000) * 100%
= (1/2) * 100%
= 50%
Option E is closest to our estimate. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
This is a case where you can get to the answer just 
by looking at the table and doing some very simple 
rounding in your head. The computer program-
ming, consultancy and related activities category is 
clearly the biggest component for both France and 
Sweden, but in France it is something above one-
third of the total (66 to 173) and in Sweden it is a half 
(37 to 74). You know therefore that the answer must 
be the closest option to one-half, which is E.

questions.question: What would have been the additional turnover (in millions of euros) from Sweden’s software publishing trade services had it been equal in percentage contribution to software publishing trade services of France?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the percentage contribution of 
software publishing in France’s trade services. We 
can then find the turnover in Sweden that would 
make its software publishing equal in percentage 
contribution to that of France. Finally, we can find 
the additional turnover required.
Calculation
The percentage contribution of software publishing 
in France’s total trade services:
= (14 003 / 173 320) * 100%
≈ (14 000 / 175 000) * 100%
= ((2 * 7000) / (25 * 7 000)) * 100%
= ((2 * 4) / (25 * 4)) * 100%
= 8%
Turnover equivalent to 8% of Sweden’s total trade 
services (in millions of euros):
= 74 175 * 8%
≈ 74 000 * (8 / 100)
= 740 * 8 
= 5 920
Additional turnover required in software publishing 
by Sweden to equal the percentage contribution 
of software publishing trade services of France (in 
millions of Euros):
= 5 920 – 5 496
= 424
Option A is the closest in value. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The options have big gaps in values. Therefore, 
approximations can be made to determine a ball 
park figure for the solution. Although our solution 
is about 74 million euros off from option A, it is still 
comfortably close enough for us to be confident.
Option C shows the total turnover, as opposed to the 
additional turnover, required to equal the percentage 
contribution of software publishing in France. 
Option D simply shows the difference between the 
turnover amounts of software publishing in both 
countries, without accounting for any percentages. 
The value in option E is obtained by adding the 
percentages of software publishing in both countries 
and then calculating a figure using Sweden’s total 
trade services.

questions.question: What would be the required additional percentage contribution from advertising if Sweden was to match France’s turnover of the same product in 2021 without altering the total amount of trade services?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the difference between the turno-
vers from advertising in France and Sweden in 2021. 
We must then convert this amount to a percentage of 
Sweden’s total turnover of trade services. 
Calculation
France’s turnover from advertising in 2021 was 12 
340 million euros and Sweden’s was 3 374 million 
euros. 
The difference between the turnovers: 
= 12 340 – 3 374
≈ 9 000 
Additional percentage contribution required from 
advertising in Sweden to match France’s turnover 
from advertising in 2021: 

235
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
= (9 000 / 74 175) * 100%
≈ (9 000 / 75 000) * 100%
= ((3 * 3 000) / (25 * 3 000)) * 100%
= (3 / 25) * 100%
= ((3 * 4) / (25 * 4)) * 100%
= 12%
Option D is the closest in value. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is very important to understand what you are 
aiming to match. In this case, we are trying to match 
the amounts and not the percentages. Options B and 
C give the current percentages of advertising in 
Sweden and France respectively. Option A gives the 
difference of these two percentages. Option E gives 
the total percentage contribution of advertising in 
Sweden after the change, not the additional.

questions.question: Based on the amounts provided in the table, which of the following equations is the correct representation of the salesman’s monthly take-home amount in terms of its components? (R = total take-home remuneration amount, M monthly basic salary amount, S = sales amount, C = commission percentage on sales, B bonus amount, T = tax-deduction percentage bef payment)

options.text: R = (M + (S * C) + B) (100% – T) f the remuneration. M = = ore

questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must construct an equation for the monthly 
take-home amount of the salesman based on the 
components of the remuneration shown in the table.
Calculation
The total take-home amount of the salesman 
comprises three main components: the basic salary 
(M), the sales commission amount and the bonus 
amount (B). The sales commission amount is a 
product of the sales amount (S) and the sales commis-
sion percentage (C). These three main components of 
the total remuneration can be expressed as follows:
M+ (S * C) + B
A tax amount must, however, be deducted before 
payment to the salesman. That tax deduction is 
made at T = 2%. It is applicable to all three main 
components of the remuneration, i.e., the tax deduc-
tion percentage must be multiplied with each one 
of them. The remaining portion, i.e., represented 
by (100% – T), of the remuneration is the take-home 
amount. Therefore, the equation for the total take-
home remuneration amount is:
R = (M + (S * C) + B) (100% – T)
We can verify our equation by substituting the data 
of January into it. 
Substitute R=4 410, M=4 000, S=100 000, C=0.50%, 
B=0 and T=2% into the equation:
4 410 = (4 000 + (100 000 * 0.50%) + 0) (100% – 2%)
4 410 = (4 000 + 500) (98%)
4 410 = 4 500 * 0.98
4 410 = 4 410
The equation has been verified.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You can skip the step of verifying the equation if you 
are short of time and confident you have constructed 
the equation correctly. However, it is essential to get 
the equation right. A simple conceptual mistake at 
this stage can result in you finding incorrect solu-
tions to all three questions in this set. 
As noted in the question, the tax deduction will be 
made on all components of the renumeration. Hence, 
the tax deduction factor must be multiplied with 
each component. Option A gives the tax deduction 
amount, not the take-home amount. Options B and 
C use the sales amount and the sales commission 
percentage as the sales commission amount respec-
tively. In option D, the tax deduction percentage is 
applied to the basic monthly salary only, which is 
incorrect.

questions.question: What was the sales commission percentage in April?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the equation established earlier and substi-
tute the given values to compute the commission 
percentage in April.
Calculation
Substitute R=5 100.90, M=4 300, S=90 000, B=500 and 
T=2% into the equation and solve for C.
R = (M + (S * C) + B) (100% - T)
5 100.90 = (4 300 + (90 000 * C) + 500) (100% - 2%)
5 100.90 = (4 800 + (90 000 * C)) (98%)
4 800 + (90 000 * C) = 5 100.90 / 98%
4 800 + (90 000 * C) = 5 100.90 / (98 / 100)
4 800 + (90 000 * C) = 5 100.90 * (100 / 98)
4 800 + (90 000 * C) = 5 205
90 000 * C = 5 205 – 4 800
90 000 * C = 405
C = 405 / 90 000
C = 0.0045 * 100%
C = 4.5%

236
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The figure for the sales commission is not given, but 
that need not be a problem. The commission amount is 
not a variable in our equation. In fact, the sales amount 
and the commission percentage are multiplied to get 
the commission amount right inside the equation.
A guess can be verified by plugging in all known 
values along with the guess into the equation to 
check if it is satisfied. Option A is half of the correct 
percentage while option E is ten times the correct 
solution.

questions.question: What was the sales amount in June (in euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
First, we must re-engineer the earlier equation. We 
have to make the tax deduction amount the subject 
of the equation instead of the take-home amount. 
We can then use the new equation and substitute the 
given values to compute the sales amount in June.
Calculation
Tax deduction amount:
= (M + (S * C) + B) * T
Substitute (Tax deduction amount) = 117.60, M=4 
300, C=0.60%, B=800 and T=2% into the equation 
and solve for S.
117.60 = (4 300 + (S * 0.60%) + 800) * 2%
117.60 = (5 100 + (0.006 * S)) * 2%
5 100 + (0.006 * S) = 117.60 / 2%
5 100 + (0.006 * S) = 5 880
0.006 * S = 5 880 – 5 100
0.006 * S = 780
S = 780 / 0.006
S = 130 000
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
We were not provided with the take-home amount 
but did not need it. We only needed to make a slight 
adjustment to our equation to use the values we do 
have.
The algebraic manipulation of the values in the equa-
tion must be carried out meticulously. However, you 
can choose to approximate values to some extent 
since each of the given options is 5 000 apart. As 
mentioned before, a guess can be verified by plug-
ging in all known values along with the guess into 
the equation to check if it is satisfied.

questions.question: How many Japanese yen would one get in exchange for 450 Czech koruna in 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the given exchange rate to convert 450 
Czech koruna into the equivalent amount of euros 
and then convert those euros into an equivalent 
number of Japanese yen based on the rate mentioned.
Calculation
The exchange rate for Czech koruna versus euro in 
2022 was:
Amount of Czech koruna / Equivalent amount of euros
= 24.566 ≈ 25
Equivalent amount of euros = 450 / 25 = 18
The exchange rate for Japanese yen versus Euro in 
2022 was:
Amount of Japanese yen / Equivalent amount of euros
= 138.03 ≈ 140
Amount of Japanese yen: = 140 * 18 = 2 520
Option D is closest in value to our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Options A and B result from algebraic mistakes. 
Option E multiplies all numbers involved and 
option C uses the rates for 2014.

questions.question: What was the sales commission percentage in April?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first use the exchange rate for 2014 to 
convert 1 000 euros into the equivalent amount in 
pounds sterling. Then we will convert those pounds 
sterling into an equivalent amount of Canadian 
dollars at the rates given for 2018.
Calculation
The exchange rate for pound sterling versus euro in 
2014 was:
Amount of pound sterling / Equivalent amount of 
euros
= 0.80612 ≈ 0.806
Amount of pound sterling
= 1 000 * 0.806 = 806

237
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
The exchange rate for Pound sterling versus euro in 
2018 was:
Amount of pound sterling / Equivalent amount of 
euros
= 0.88471 ≈ 0.885
Equivalent amount of euros:
806 / 0.885 ≈ 800 / 0.9 ≈ 890
The exchange rate for the Canadian dollar versus the 
euro in 2018 was:
Amount of Canadian dollars / Equivalent amount of 
euros
= 1.5294 ≈ 1.5
Amount of Canadian dollars: = 890 * 1.5 = 1 335 
Option D is closest in value to our estimate. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
A shortcut method:
1 000 * 0.80612 * (1.5294 / 0.88471) = 1 393.54

questions.question: What was the sales amount in June (in euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the charged fee amount and the fee 
rate to evaluate the equivalent sum of the euros 
amounts in both transactions. We can then calcu-
late the amount of Bulgarian lev the customer had 
to begin with. 
Calculation
Two exchange transactions were carried out by the 
dealer. One from Bulgarian lev to euros and then the 
other from euros to Romanian leu.
The fee (in euros) for each transaction must therefore 
be:
25.56 / 2 = 12.78
We know that:
Dealer fees amount:
= Fee percentage * Equivalent amount of euros
= 12.78 = 0.5% * Equivalent amount of euros
Equivalent amount of euros:
= 12.78 / 0.5% = 2 556
The exchange rate for Bulgarian lev versus euro in 
2018 was:
Amount of Bulgarian lev / Equivalent amount of euros
= 1.9558 ≈ 1.96
Amount of Bulgarian lev: = 2 556 * 1.96 ≈ 5009
Our estimate is closest to option C. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must be careful to read the data for the relevant 
currency and year. In this question, the exchange 
rate for the Romanian leu is irrelevant. Option E 
provides the equivalent amount of the Romanian leu 
in 2018. Options C and D are pretty close together in 
value. Hence, over-approximation might result in a 
value that is closer to the incorrect option.
A simple shortcut is to divide the value in the guessed 
option by the exchange rate for the Bulgarian lev 
in 2018 and then apply the 0.5% transaction fee to 
verify the option.

questions.question: What was the percentage change in road traffic in the Netherlands from 2019 to 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the percentage change in road 
traffic in the Netherlands in 2021 relative to 2019.
Calculation
Road traffic (in VKM) in the Netherlands in 2019 
was 140 709.
Road traffic (in VKM) in the Netherlands in 2021 was 
123 105.
The percentage change in road traffic in the 
Netherlands from 2019 to 2021:
= ((123 105 – 140 709) / 140 709) * 100%
≈ ((122 500 – 140 000) / 140 000) * 100%
= (–17 500 / 140 000) * 100%
= (– 1 /8) * 100%
= –12.5%
Option B matches our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
This was a fairly straightforward question but as 
always you must be careful to use the correct data. 
Using the incorrect year may result in a solution 
like option E (relative to 2020). In option A, the 
percentage change was found relative to the value 
of 2021.

238
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: Given that approximately 92.5% of the road traffic in France was from vehicles registered in the country, what was the change in road traffic owing to vehicles registered elsewhere from 2020 to 2021 (in VKM)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must determine the percentage of road traffic 
from vehicles not registered in France. We can then 
use this percentage to determine the road traffic (in 
VKM) in 2020 and 2021 from vehicles not registered 
in France.
Calculation
The percentage of road traffic from vehicles not 
registered in France: 
= 100% – (Percentage of road traffic from vehicles not 
registered in France)
= 100% – 92.5%
= 7.5%
Change in road traffic (in VKM) from 2020 to 2021 
owing to vehicles not registered in France:
= ((Road traffic in France in 2021) – (Road traffic in 
France in 2020)) * 7.5% 
= (562 267 – 524 554) * 7.5%
≈ 38 000 * 7.5%
= 38 000 * 1% * 7 + 38 000 * 0.5%
= 380 * 7 + 190
= 2 660 + 190
= +2 850
Option C is clearly the closest in value. 
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You can get to this answer simply by thinking about 
the information you are given. The table tells us 
that road traffic increased from 2020 to 2021, which 
immediately rules out answer options A and B, 
which require a decrease. You can also see that the 
increase was approximately (562 000 – 525 000) = 
37 000. However, the question text tells us that of 
this 37 000 increase only 7.5% was due to vehicles 
registered elsewhere, so it is obvious that D (nearly 
35 000) and E (nearly 38 000) cannot be right either. 
Only C is left.
Options A and B give the changes in road traffic due 
to vehicles registered in the country and outside 
the country respectively from 2019 to 2021. Option 
D gives the change in road traffic from 2020 to 2021 
owing to vehicles registered in France. Option E 
simply states the change in all road traffic in France 
from 2020 to 2021.

questions.question: The share of all road traffic in Spain due to lorries increased by 1.5% from 2019 to 2020. Given that road traffic due to lorries in 2020 was 22 410 VKM, what was the road traffic in Spain in 2019 due to all vehicles other than lorries (in VKM)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first work out the percentage of road traffic 
in Spain due to lorries in 2020. We can then deter-
mine the percentage of road traffic in Spain due to 
lorries in 2019 by using the given percentage change. 
Finally, by using the percentage of road traffic not 
caused by lorries in 2019, we can calculate the road 
traffic in Spain in 2019 due to all vehicles other than 
lorries (in VKM).
Calculation
The percentage of road traffic in Spain due to lorries 
in 2020:
= (22 410 / 195 687) * 100%
≈ 11.5%
The percentage of road traffic in Spain due to lorries 
in 2019:
= 11.5% – 1.5% = 10%
The percentage of road traffic in Spain not due to 
lorries in 2019:
= 100% – 10% = 90%
Road traffic in Spain not due to lorries in 2019 (in 
VKM):
= 252 055 * 90%
≈ 250 000 * 90%
= 225 000
Option D is the closest in value to our approximate 
solution. Hence, it is probably correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Approximating made the calculation a bit quicker, 
though not without some risk as answer options 
D and E are close together. Under test conditions 
judging when, and when not, to approximate can 
often be the critical first step in finding the correct 
answer.

questions.question: What share of the pets in the European Union in 2022 did the cats in France and Ita account for?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
The number of cats in France and Italy in 2022 must be 
added together and then divided by the total number of 
pets in the EU in 2022 to find the required percentage.

239
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Calculation
Total number of cats in France and Italy in 2022 (in 
millions):
= 14.90 + 10.23
= 25.13
The total number of pets in the EU in 2022 (in thou-
sands) was 213 363.
Percentage share of cats in France and Italy in the 
total number of pets in the EU in 2022:
= ((25.13 * 1 000 000) / (213 363 * 1 000)) * 100%
= (25 130 / 213 363) * 100%
≈ 11.8%
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The total number of cats in France and Italy is 
roughly 15 + 10 = 25 million while the total number 
of pets in the EU is almost 215 million. That is 
slightly more than 10% and hence option D is the 
obvious candidate if you need to make a guess. 
Options A, B, C and E are the result of incorrect selec-
tions of types of pet and countries.

questions.question: Given that the Netherlands had 2.3% of all cats and dogs in the European Union in 2022, how many dogs (in thousands) did the Netherlands have in 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the given percentage to calculate the 
sum of cats and dogs in the Netherland in 2022. We 
can then subtract the given number of cats in the 
Netherlands to determine the number of dogs in the 
Netherlands in 2022.
Calculation
Let N, be the sum of cats and dogs in the Netherlands 
in 2022 (in thousands).
The Netherlands had 2.3% of all cats and dogs in the 
European Union in 2022:
(N / (77 928 + 66 866)) * 100% = 2.3%
N / (78000 + 67 000) ≈ 2.3% / 100%= 0.023
N / 145 000 = 0.023
N = 0.023 * 145 000
N = 3 335
Number of dogs (in thousands) in the Netherlands 
in 2022:
= 3 335 – 1.90 * 1 000
= 3 335 – 1 900
= 1 435
Option C is the closest to our estimated value.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You must take care to read the data for cats and dogs 
in the EU and the number of cats in the Netherlands 
only. Option B gives the solution for France in 2019. 
Options B (in millions) and E (in thousands) provide 
the solution based on the percentage of 2.3% relative 
to the total number of pets in the EU. Option A has 
an incorrect order of magnitude. Option D is simply 
the number of cats, which is already known.

questions.question: Sales of pet food in the European Union generated a turnover of 29.1 billion euros in 2022. Assuming uniform consumption of pe food by every pet in the EU, how much pet food (in millions of euros) was consumed b pet dogs in Romania in 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the consumption of pet food by 
every pet in the EU based on the given pet food 
turnover and multiply that with the number of dogs 
in Romania to evaluate the pet food (in millions of 
euros) consumed by pet dogs in Romania in 2022.
Calculation
Consumption of pet food by every pet in the EU (in 
euros):
= (29.1 * 1 000 000 000) / (213 363 * 1 000)
= 29 100 000 / 213 363
≈ 136
Pet food (in millions of euros) consumed by pet dogs 
in Romania in 2022:
= 136 * 4.19
≈ 570
Option C is closest in value to our estimate and 
hence is our best guess.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The orders of magnitude of every number are crucial 
to our solution. Option E provides the solution in 
thousands of euros. Option A is the consumption 
of pet food by every pet in the EU (given in euros). 
Option C uses the number of cats in Romania 
instead of the number of dogs. Option D results 
from dividing the entire pet food turnover by the 
number of pet dogs in Romania.
Although the assumption that each pet in the EU 
consumes the same value of pet food may seem 
absurd, you must always stick to the information 
given in the question.

240
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: Which of the following cities had the lowest total income in 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must estimate the total incomes of each of the 
given five U.S. cities to determine which of them has 
the lowest total income.
Calculation
Per capita income (in U.S. dollars):
= (Total income, in thousands of U.S. dollars) / 
(Resident population, in thousands)
Total income, in thousands of U.S. dollars:
= Per capita income (in U.S. dollars) * (Resident 
population, in thousands)
Total income (in thousands of U.S. dollars) in 2022:
El Paso: 678.42 * 25 670 ≈ 700 * 26 000 = 18 200 000
Jacksonville: 954.61 * 32 953 ≈ 1 000 * 33 000 = 33 000 
000
Las Vegas: 646.79 * 33 825 ≈ 650 * 34 000 = 22 100 000
New York: 8 467.51 * 42 406 ≈ 8 500 * 42 000 = 357 000 
000
Philadelphia: 1 576.25 * 32 707 ≈ 1 600 * 33 000 = 52 
800 000
Therefore, El Paso had the lowest total income in 
2022.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It’s important in a question like this not to get 
distracted by irrelevant data. There are 15 cities in 
the table but only five of them are relevant to the 
question.
Above we have the full calculations for each of the 
five cities. However, you can get to the answer just 
by thinking about the information in the table. The 
per capita income column tells you that El Paso has 
the lowest per capita income. The resident popula-
tion column shows that only Las Vegas has a smaller 
population than El Paso, but the difference in popu-
lation between the two is very small whereas the 
difference in per capita income is large. The answer 
must be El Paso.

questions.question: Given that the total income of Los Angeles was U.S. dollars 35.5 billion more than that of Chicago in 2021, what was the per capita income of Los Angeles in 2021 (in U.S. dollars)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
First, we must calculate the total income for Chicago 
in 2021. Then, by using the mentioned difference we 
can evaluate the total income of Los Angeles. Finally, 
by using the per capita formula, we can calculate the 
per capita income of Los Angeles.
Calculation
Total income, in thousands of U.S. dollars:
= Per capita income (in U.S. dollars) * (Resident 
population, in thousands)
Total income of Chicago in 2021 (in thousands of U.S. 
dollars):
= 2 696.56 * 43 217
≈ 2 700 * 43 000
= 116 100 000
Total income of Los Angeles in 2021 (in billions of 
U.S. dollars):
= 116.1 + 35.5
= 151.6
Per capita income (in U.S. dollars):
= (Total income, in thousands of U.S. dollars) / 
(Resident population, in thousands)
Per capita income of Los Angeles in 2021 (in U.S. 
dollars):
= (151.6 * 1 000 000 000) / (3 849.30 * 1 000)
≈ 151 600 000 / 3 800
≈ 39 900
Based on our estimate, option D is the correct answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is essential to keep track of the order of magni-
tudes in every step of the calculation. We must also 
be careful to read the data for Chicago and Los 
Angeles only. If by mistake the difference in total 
incomes is treated as the total income of Los Angeles, 
your solution will equal option A. If 35.5 billion U.S. 
dollars is subtracted from Chicago’s total income, 
instead of being added, your solution will match 
option B. Option E, results when the total income of 
Los Angeles is divided by Chicago’s population. 

241
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Option C is close to the correct answer option D, so 
may look tempting for a guess. However, you might 
notice that it is simply Los Angeles’ population 
stated in hundreds.

questions.question: Given that the total income of Houston was between those of Chicago and San Francisco, what was the resident population (in thousands) of Houston in 2021?

options.text: Cannot be determined with the given information

questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the range of the total income of 
Houston to check if the resident population can be 
evaluated by using the per capita income.
Calculation
Resident population = Total income / Per capita 
income
For us to exactly calculate any one of the three vari-
ables in this formula, we must know the exact values 
of the other two. We do know the exact value for the 
per capita income for Houston, but we only know 
a range for the total income, i.e., that it is between 
those of Chicago and San Francisco. Therefore, it is 
impossible to determine the exact value for Houston’s 
resident population. However, we will try to narrow 
down the options by using the range of total income 
to find the range of resident population.
The resident population upper limit of Houston by 
using the total income of Chicago (in thousands):
= (2 696.56 * 43 217) / 36 265
≈ (2 700 * 43 000) / 36 000
= 3 225
The resident population lower limit of Houston 
by using the total income of San Francisco (in 
thousands):
= (815.20 * 80 383) / 36 265
≈ (800 * 80 000) / 36 000
= 1 775
Options B, C and D all fall in the approximate range 
of population of Houston. Therefore, we cannot 
select any one of them and Option E must be the 
correct answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Even if we had found only one match in the range of 
population of Houston, we still could not say with 
certainty that it is the correct solution. You have to 
know the exact total income and the exact per capita 
income to find the exact population of Houston. 
Option C is the actual population of Houston, but it is 
not possible to determine it using the given informa-
tion. Options A, B and D are just random numbers.

questions.question: Which of these countries had the second- largest increase in the balance of payments in international trade in services with EU27 countries from 2020 to 2022?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must determine the change in each coun-
try’s balance of payments in international trade in 
services with EU27 countries from 2020 to 2022 to 
find which country had the second-largest increase.
Calculation
We must subtract the balance of payment figures of 
2020 (with EU27 countries) from those of 2022 (with 
EU27 countries) to evaluate the change.
Bulgaria: 
= 2 295 – 1 802 ≈ 2 300 – 1 800 = 500
Finland:
= –9 747 – (–6 276) ≈ –9 750 + 6250 = –3 500
Germany:
= –58 130 – (–19 171) ≈ –58 000 + 19 000 = –39 000
Poland:
= 18 281 – 12 463 ≈ 18 000 – 12 500 = 5 500
Serbia:
= 505 – 232 ≈ 500 – 230 = 270
As per our approximate calculations, Bulgaria had 
the second-largest increase.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is very important to use the data for international 
trade in services with EU27 countries in 2020 and 
2022 only. If data for Extra-EU27 countries is used 
instead, our answer will change to Poland. If we use 
the data for 2021 instead of 2022, our answer will 
match option E, i.e., Serbia. It is also important to 
remember in cases like Germany, when subtracting 
with negative numbers, that the operator must 
change to an addition sign, as shown above.
You don’t actually need to do all the calculations 
shown above. Look at the table. As we are looking 
for an increase, i.e., a positive change, from 2020 to 
2022 Finland and Germany cannot possibly be right 
because their change was negative. The increase 
alone in Poland (from 12 463 to 18 281) was visibly 
bigger than the total for 2022 for both Bulgaria and 
Serbia, so Poland is clearly in first place. That leaves 
only Bulgaria and Serbia to calculate. 

242
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

questions.question: What was the overall change in the balance of payments in international trade in services for Germany from 2021 to 2022?

options.text: Imports grew 29.53 billion euros more than its exports
Imports grew 27.12 billion euros more than its exports
Imports grew 21.95 billion euros more than its exports
Imports grew 5.17 billion euros more than its exports
Exports grew 9.71 billion euros more than its imports

questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We can calculate the overall change in the balance 
of payments in international trade in services for 
Germany from 2021 to 2022 by adding the figures 
for both balance of payments in 2022 and then 
subtracting the sum of both balance of payments 
figures of 2020 from them.
Calculation
Overall balance of payments in international trade 
in services for Germany in 2022 (in millions of 
euros):
= (Balance of payments in international trade in 
services with EU27 countries) + (Balance of payments 
in international trade in services with Extra-EU27 
countries)
= –58 130 + 31 321
≈ – 58 000 + 31 000
= –27 000
Overall balance of payments in international trade in 
services for Germany in 2021 (in millions of Euros):
= (Balance of payments in international trade in 
services with EU27 countries) + (Balance of payments 
in international trade in services with Extra-EU27 
countries)
= –36 180 + 36 494 
≈ –36 000 + 36 500
= 500
Overall change in the balance of payments in inter-
national trade in services for Germany from 2021 to 
2022 (in millions of euros):
= – 27 000 – 500
= – 27 500
Option B is the closest in value. Hence, it is probably 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is crucial to use the data for international trade 
in services for Germany with EU27 and Extra-EU27 
countries in 2021 and 2022 only. If data for 2020 is 
used instead of 2021, your solution would be the 
incorrect option A. Similarly, if you used just the 
data for EU27 countries or for Extra-EU27 coun-
tries your solutions would match options C and D 
respectively. Option E uses the data for Poland.

questions.question: The sum of imports by Finland, Poland and Serbia in international trade in services from Extra-EU27 countries increased by 3 584 million euros from 2020 to 2021. What was the increase in the sum of exports by Finland, Poland and Serbia in international trade in services to Extra-EU27 countries from 2020 to 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
First, we must determine the change in the sum of 
balance of payments of Finland, Poland and Serbia 
with Extra-EU27 countries from 2020 to 2021. Then, 
we must use the given change in the sum of imports 
by these countries from Extra-EU27 countries to find 
the sum of change in the exports from these coun-
tries to the Extra-EU27 countries from 2020 to 2021.
Calculation
The sum of the balance of payments of Finland, 
Poland and Serbia with Extra-EU27 countries in 
2021 (in millions of euros):
= 4 991 + 11 551 + 939
≈ 5 000 + 11 500 + 950
= 17 450
The sum of the balance of payments of Finland, 
Poland and Serbia with Extra-EU27 countries in 2020 
(in millions of euros):
= 3 706 + 10 493 + 870
≈ 3 700 + 10 500 + 850
= 15 050
The change in the sum of the balance of payments of 
Finland, Poland and Serbia with Extra-EU27 coun-
tries from 2020 to 2021 (in millions of euros):
= 17 450 – 15 050
= 2 400
We know that: 
Balance of payments = Exports – Imports
Hence: Exports = Balance of payments + Imports
The increase in the sum of exports by Finland, 
Poland and Serbia in international trade in services 
to Extra-EU27 countries from 2020 to 2021 (in 
millions of euros):
= 2 400 + 3 584
= 5 984
Option C is therefore likely to be correct based on 
our approximate solution.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You have to be careful to rearrange the balance 
of payments equation correctly. You also must 
be careful to read the data for 2020 and 2021 for 
Finland, Poland and Serbia only with Extra-EU27 

243
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
countries. If you read the data for 2022 instead of 
2021, options D and E will match solutions for the 
change in balance of payments and exports. Option 
A is simply the change in the balance of payments 
from 2020 to 2021. Option B gives the change in 
exports of the three countries to the EU27 countries 
from 2020 to 2021.

questions.question: What was the total distance travelled by Sunshine (in km)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the formula for average speed to calcu-
late the total distance travelled by Sunshine.
Calculation
Average speed of Sunshine (in km/h):
= 16 * 1.852
≈ 30
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
Hence: Total distance = Average speed * Total time
Total distance travelled by Sunshine (in hours):
= 30 * 8.5
= 255
Option D is close enough to our approximate solu-
tion to be correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
This is a fairly simple question. However, it is vital 
to change the given speed of Sunshine from knots 
to km/h because the solution is required in km. You 
must also use the given time taken for Sunshine and 
not for Moonlight or your solution will equal option 
C. The average speed formula must be re-arranged 
properly, otherwise you might end up with option 
A. Option E gives the solution in meters, instead of 
km.

questions.question: What was Moonlight’s average speed (in knots)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
First, we must compute the time taken by Moonlight 
and then use the distance and time to calculate the 
average speed in km/h. We can then use the conver-
sion factor to find the average speed in knots.
Calculation
Total time taken by Moonlight (in hours):
= 8.5 – 1.5
= 7
Average speed = Total distance / Total time
Average speed of Moonlight (in km/h):
= 324.1 / 7
= 46.3
Average speed of Moonlight (in knots):
= 46.3 / 1.852
= 25
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The correct use of the average speed formula is as 
important for this question as the conversion of 
units. You must be careful not to add the 1.5 hours 
to the 8.5 hours of Sunshine or your solution will 
match option A. Option D gives the average speed 
of Moonlight in km/h. Option E gives the distance 
between port Best and port Crown in nautical miles.

questions.question: Both ships departed at the same time from port Crown to return to their respective ports of origin. On average, Moonlight travelled 5 knots faster than Sunshine. Given that Sunshine arrived back at port Ace at the same time as Moonlight reached port Best, what was the total time it took each of them?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Use the known distances from port Crown to port 
Best and to port Ace along with the speed and time 
differences to construct an equation in terms of the 
time taken by Moonlight. Then, solve the equation.
Calculation
Let T, be the time taken by each of Sunshine and 
Moonlight to travel to port Ace and port Best respec-
tively (in hours).
Average speed of Sunshine from port Crown to port 
Ace (in km/h):
= 251.9 / T
Average speed of Moonlight from port Crown to 
port Best (in km/h):
= 324.1 / T
The difference in the average speeds of both ships 
(in km/h):
= 5 * 1.852

244
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Equation for the difference of average speeds of both 
ships:
(324.1 / T) – (251.9 / T) = 5 * 1.852
(324.1 – 251.9) / T = 9.26
72.2 / T = 9.26
T = 72.2 / 9.26
T ≈ 7 .796 hours
T ≈ 7 hours, 48 minutes
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Correct construction of the equation is key to this 
problem. Since the option values are quite close 
together it is better in this case not to approximate 
any numbers unless absolutely necessary.

questions.question: On average, how many more smartphone mobile network subscriptions per smartpho sold were there in 2022 as compared to 2018
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must separately find the ratios of smartphone 
mobile network subscriptions per smartphone sold, 
for 2018 and 2022. We can then subtract the two to 
find the difference.
Calculation
Smartphone mobile network subscriptions per 
smartphone sold in 2018:
= 4 906.90 / 1 556.27
≈ 4 950 / 1 500
= (33 * 150) / (10 * 150)
= 33 / 10
≈ 3.3
Smartphone mobile network subscriptions per 
smartphone sold in 2022:
= 6 421.78 / 1 395.25
≈ 6 400 / 1 400
= (32 * 200) / (7 * 200)
= 32 / 7
≈ 4.6
Difference in smartphone mobile network subscrip-
tions per smartphone sold in 2022 as compared to 
2018:
= 4.6 – 3.3 
= 1.3
Option B is closest to our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Both the number of smartphone mobile network 
subscriptions and the number of smartphones 
sold are given in millions. Hence, when we calcu-
late the ratio, the millions cancel each other out. It 
is important to calculate the ratio correctly. Option 
A gives the difference of the reciprocals (of what is 
required). Option C gives the difference of ratios for 
2022 and 2016. Options D and E give the values for 
the ratios in 2018 and 2022 respectively.

questions.question: What was the forecast increase in monthly mobile data traffic due to smartphones sold 2023 relative to 2016 (in thousands of TB)? 1 TB = 1 000 GB
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must separately find monthly mobile data traffic 
due to smartphones sold, in 2016 and 2022. We can 
then subtract the two to find the increase.
Calculation
Mobile data traffic due to smartphones sold in 2016 
(in thousands of TB):
= (1.97 * (1 495.96 * 1 000 000)) / (1 000 * 1 000)
≈ 2 * 1 500
= 3 000
Mobile data traffic due to smartphones sold in 2023 
(in thousands of TB):
= (20.37 * (1 339.51 * 1 000 000)) / (1 000 * 1 000)
≈ 20 * 1 350
= 27 000
Increase in mobile data traffic due to smartphones 
sold in 2023 as compared to 2016 (in thousands of 
TB):
= 27 000 – 3 000 
= 24 000
Option C is the closest in value. Hence, it is probably 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You only need the mobile data traffic and smart-
phones sold data for 2016 and 2023 for this question. 
You must bear in mind that the number of smart-
phones sold is given in millions and the mobile 
data traffic ratio is per smartphone. Hence, you 
must multiply the numerator by a million to get the 
figures right. The value in option B was achieved by 
using the data for 2022 instead of 2023. Options A 
and D provide the mobile data traffic due to smart-
phones sold in 2016 and 2023 respectively.
If pressed for time, you can get close enough with 
some very crude approximating in your head to find 
the first two digits of the answer. For 2016 do 1.5 * 2 = 

245
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
3. For 2023 do 1.3 * 20 = 26. Then you have 26 – 3 = 23, 
but as both numbers for 2023 were rounded down, 
you know the actual answer might quite likely start 
with 24 not 23. However, both options C and E have 
24 as their first two digits and at this point you have 
to get the order of magnitude right. The conver-
sion factor of a TB of data in terms of GB has been 
provided and the solution is required in thousands of 
TB. If you miss this, and instead calculate in TB, your 
answer will match option E.

questions.question: How many more, or less, smartphones are forecast to be sold worldwide in 2025 as compared to 2021 (in million units)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first observe the pattern of change in the 
number of smartphones sold each year. We can then 
determine if there is a trend to enable us to extrapo-
late to forecast the number of smartphone sales in 
2025. If there is, we can then find the difference with 
the figure for 2021.
Consider the following regarding the number of 
smartphones sold each year:
From 2019 to 2020 there was a decrease of 189 million
From 2020 to 2021 there was an increase of 82 million
From 2021 to 2022 there was a decrease of 39 million
From 2022 to 2023 there is a (forecast) decrease of 56 
million 
The numbers show great and seemingly random 
fluctuations. There is no other data provided to 
support a reliable interpretation or indicate any sort 
of pattern. We must conclude that the answer is E, 
Cannot say.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The risk with this question is that you embark on 
all sorts of complicated calculations whereas there 
is no reliable way of producing a forecast. To do so, 
would require additional information to explain the 
apparently random pattern in recent years, and also 
forecasts of market trends for the future.

questions.question: What was the loan amount (principal amount plus accrued interest amount) on 31 December 2023 (in euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the given equation by substituting the 
principal amount, the annual interest rate and the 
number of years to find the future value of the loan 
after 2 years.
Calculation
Substitute P = 10 000, r = 3% and t = 2 into the given 
equation to evaluate A.
A = P(1 + r)t
A = 10 000 × (1 + 3%)2
A = 10 000 × (1.03)2
A = 10 000 × 1.0609
A = 10 609
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
The number of years between the dates is two. If 
the calculation is done for 3 years, the solution will 
match option E. Option B gives the solution for just 
1 year. 
You can solve this question in your head. 3% of 10 
000 is 300. Using simple interest, that comes to 600 in 
interest for 2 years. Option C gives a solution (10 600) 
based on simple interest. However, the information 
we are given tells us that compound interest is charged. 
Compounding increases the interest amount slightly 
since interest on the previous interest amount is also 
charged. Hence, option D (10 609) should be the 
obvious choice.

questions.question: On which of the following dates will the future loan amount be equal to 11 592.74 euros?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the given equation by substituting 
the given future value, the principal amount and 
the annual interest rate to find the number of years 
and hence the date at which the loan value equals 11 
592.74 euros.
Calculation
Substitute A = 11 592.74, P = 10 000 and r = 3% into 
the given equation to evaluate t.
A = P(1 + r)t
11 592.74 = 10 000 × (1 + 3%)t
(1.03)t = (11 592.74) / (10 000)
(1.03)t = 1.159274)
We can substitute values of t, according to the given 
options to verify each one.
For 31 December 2024: t = 3
(1.03)3 = 1.159274
1.092727 ≠ 1.159274

246
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
For 31 December 2025:t = 4
(1.03)4 = 1.159274
1.12550881 ≠ 1.159274
For 31 December 2026: t = 5
(1.03)5 = 1.159274
1.1592740743 ≈ 1.159274
Therefore, 31 December 2026 is the date when the 
loan amount equals 11 592.74 euros.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
A good strategy would have been to start with the 
date in the middle, i.e., option C, and then moved 
towards option B or D depending on the resulting 
value. This would have cut down our calculating 
time (and in this case produced the correct answer 
immediately). 
It is important to substitute values with great care 
and not make approximations because the difference 
between each value is just 3%.

questions.question: If Mr. Long is able to pay off the loan amount (principal amount plus accrued interest amount) by 31 December 2029, what would be his total savings in interest expense (in euros)?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must use the given equation by substituting the 
principal amount, the annual interest rate and the 
number of years to find the future value of the loan 
after 8 years and then after 10 years. We can then 
find the difference of the two values to determine 
the total savings.
Calculation
Future loan value on 31 December 2029, i.e., after 8 
years:
Substitute P = 10 000, r = 3% and t = 8 into the given 
equation to evaluate A8.
A8 = P(1 + r)t
A8 = 10 000 × (1 + 3%)8
A8 = 10 000 × (1.03)8
A8 ≈ 10 000 × 1.266770
A8 = 12 667.70
Future loan value on 31 December 2031, i.e., after 10 
years:
Substitute P = 10 000, r = 3% and t = 10 into the given 
equation to evaluate A10.
A10 = P(1 + r)t
A10 = 10 000 × (1 + 3%)10
A10 = 10 000 × (1.03)10
A10 ≈ 10 000 × 1.343916
A10 = 13 439.16 
Total savings, i.e. the difference in future values, due 
to early repayment:
A10 – A8
= 13 439.16 – 12 667.70
= 771.46
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
If you calculate the difference in interest amounts on 
the basis of simple interest your solution will match 
option B. It would also be a mistake to assume 
that the savings will be equal to the compounded 
interest amount for the first two years because the 
compounding in the 9th and 10th years will be 
higher than the first two years. Option C gives the 
solution for such a mistake. Option D is the differ-
ence of the future values had the loan been for 9 
years and was repaid early in 7 years. 
As mentioned above, the compounding in the 9th 
and 10th years will be higher than the first two years. 
Hence, options D and E are the only possible candi-
dates for a guess, giving you a 50/50 chance.

questions.question: Given that the total number of people with a disability in Belgium was around five times the total number of people with a disability in Slovenia, what was the ratio of people with heart, blood pressure or circulation problems in Belgium to those in Slovenia?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Read the given percentages of people with a disa-
bility having heart, blood pressure or circulation 
problems in Belgium and Slovenia. Use these figures 
and the given ratio of the number of people with a 
disability in both countries to find the required ratio.
Calculation
Let S be the number of people in Slovenia who had 
a disability.
The number of people with a disability in Belgium:
= 5 * S = 5S
The number of people with a disability having heart, 
blood pressure or circulation problems in Belgium:
= (5S) * 9.6%
= 0.48 * S

247
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Similarly, in Slovenia:
= S * 14.1%
= 0.141 * S
The ratio of people with heart, blood pressure or 
circulation problems in Belgium to those in Slovenia:
= (0.48 * S) / (0.141 * S)
= 0.48 / 0.141
≈ 49 / 14 
= 7 /2
= 3.50
Option C is the closest in value to our estimated ratio.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Option B simply gives the ratio of the percentages 
from the chart while option E gives the ratio of the 
number of people with disabilities in both countries. 
Option A is the reciprocal of the correct solution, 
i.e., the numerator and the denominator of the ratio 
were swapped.
You can guess the right answer fairly simply given 
that the answer options are sufficiently spread. The 
percentage of people with heart, blood pressure or 
circulation problems in Belgium is lower than that 
in Slovenia. However, the number of people with 
disabilities in Belgium is 5 times that in Slovenia. 
Hence, 5 * 9.6% = 48%. This is between 3 to 4 times 
greater than the 14.1% of Slovenia. Therefore, option 
C would be the best choice for an answer.

questions.question: If the total number of people with a disability in Belgium was the same as the total number of people with a disability in Greece, what was the ratio of people with a disability not having arthrosis, diabetes and skin conditions in Greece with respect to Belgium?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Read the given percentages of people with a disa-
bility having arthrosis, diabetes and skin conditions 
in Belgium and Greece. Use these figures and the 
given ratio of the number of people with a disability 
in both countries to find the required ratio.
Calculation
Let G, be the number of people in Greece who had 
a disability. According to the question text, the 
number of people with a disability in Belgium is the 
same, so is also G.
The number of people with disability not having 
arthrosis, diabetes and skin conditions in Belgium:
= G * (100%– (27.7% + 5.9%))
= G * (100% – 33.6%)
= G * 66.4%
= 0.664 * G
Similarly, in Greece:
= G * (100% – (8.2% + 8.4%))
= G * (100% – 16.6%)
= G * 83.4%
= 0.834 * G
The ratio of people with a disability not having 
arthrosis, diabetes and skin conditions in Greece to 
those in Belgium:
= (0.834 * G) / (0.664 * G)
= 0.834 / 0.664
≈ 0.83 / 0.664 
= 1.25
Option D is the closest in value to our estimated 
ratio.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
It is essential to read the percentages for arthrosis, 
diabetes and skin conditions in Belgium and Greece 
only. We could have alternatively read the percent-
ages of all other disabilities excluding the two noted 
ones, but that is simply a waste of time.
We used the same variable for both the number of 
people with disabilities so that they can conveniently 
be cancelled out during the calculations.
Option A is the ratio of the percentages from the 
chart while option C gives the ratio of the number of 
people with disabilities in both countries. Option B is 
the reciprocal of the correct solution, i.e., the numer-
ator and the denominator of the ratio were swapped. 
Option E is the reciprocal of option A.
The percentage of people with disabilities in Greece 
not having the said conditions was higher than in 
Belgium. Hence, you know that the ratio will be 
greater than 1. This leaves you with options D and E 
as candidates for a guess, if required.

questions.question: Assuming that the number of people with disabilities in Belgium and Greece are equa and each is five times the number of people with disabilities in Slovenia, what percenta of people with a disability in these three countries had “Other longstanding health problems”?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
Read the given percentages of people with a disa-
bility having other longstanding health problems 
in Belgium, Greece and Slovenia. Use these figures 
and the given ratios of the number of people with a 
disability in the three countries to find the required 
ratio.

248
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Calculation
Let S be the number of people in Slovenia who had a 
disability. The number of people with a disability in 
both Belgium and Greece is the same: 5S
The number of people with other longstanding 
health problems in Belgium:
= (5S) * 11.0% = 0.55 * S
In Greece: = (5S) * 8.9% = 0.445 * S
In Slovenia: = S * 11.7% = 0.117 * S
The total number of people with disabilities in 
Belgium, Greece and Slovenia:
= 5S + 5S + S = 11S
The percentage of people with a disability in these 
three countries who had other longstanding health 
problems:
= ((0.55S + 0.445S + 0.117S) / 11S) * 100%
= (1.112 / 11) * 100%
≈ 10.1%
Option C is the closest in value to our estimated ratio.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
As always, you must be careful to read the data that 
is required as it is easy to go wrong on a compli-
cated bar chart. It is also very important to express 
all numbers in terms of a single variable so that the 
variable can be cancelled out eventually.
Options A, D and E are simply the percentages for 
other longstanding health problems for each of the 
three individual countries, as shown on the chart. 
It would be an unlikely coincidence if the overall 
percentage for the three countries combined was 
exactly equal to any one of the individual percentages. 
One of options B or C would therefore be your best 
choice for a guess, if required.

questions.question: What was the change in the percentage share of electricity generated in Germany using solid fossil fuels from 2017 to 2019?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must calculate the percentage share of electricity 
generated in Germany using solid fossil fuels in 
both 2017 and 2019 and then subtract the two to find 
the change.
Calculation
Percentage share of electricity generated in Germany 
using solid fossil fuels in 2017:
= (241 254 / 652 022) * 100%
≈ 37%
And in 2019:
= (171 450 / 605 430) * 100%
≈ 28%
The change in the percentage share of electricity 
generated in Germany using solid fossil fuels from 
2017 to 2019:
= 28% – 37%
= –9%
Option C is the closest to our estimate.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
Care is needed in reading the data. Option D gives 
the value of change in percentage share from 2019 to 
2021. The value in option A is the percentage change 
in the electricity generated using solid fossil fuels, 
without any reference to the total electricity gener-
ated in Germany. Option B is the percentage share 
change relative to the total electricity generated in 
2017 only. Option E is simply the percentage share of 
electricity generated using solid fossil fuels in 2019 
in Germany.

questions.question: How much more electricity (in GWh) must Poland make from renewables and biofuels by replacing other sources, relative to the total generation in 2021, to match the percentage share of electricity generated in Türkiye from renewables and biofuels in 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must determine the percentage share of elec-
tricity generation from renewables and biofuels 
in Türkiye in 2021. We must then find the desired 
level of electricity generation from renewables and 
biofuels in Poland by using this desired percentage 
with its total generation. Finally, we can compute 
the additional generation required from renewables 
and biofuels to match the percentage share of elec-
tricity generated in Türkiye from renewables and 
biofuels in 2021.
Calculation
The percentage share of electricity generation from 
renewables and biofuels in Türkiye in 2021: 
= (118 552 / 333 412) * 100%
≈ 35.5%

249
numerical reasoning mixed questions test
Desired level of electricity generation from renewa-
bles and biofuels in Poland in 2021 (in GWh):
= 35.5% * 179 548
≈ 35.5% * 180 000
= 63 900
Additional electricity generation required from 
renewables and biofuels to match the level of Türkiye 
(in GWh): 
= 63 900 – 31 330
≈ 63 900 – 31 300
= 32 600
Option D is the closest in value. Hence, it is probably 
correct.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You can make a good guess using simple frac-
tions. Approximately a third (≈ 111 000 / 333 000) 
of Türkiye’s generation is from renewables and 
biofuels. Poland’s one-third generation would be ≈ 
180 000 / 3 = 60 000, out of which ≈ 30 000 is already 
in place. Therefore, you know that something like an 
additional 30 000 GWh is required. Options C and 
D are close to this. However, option C is given in 
the table as the actual 2021 production for renewa-
bles and biofuels for Poland. That just leaves D as a 
good choice.
Option A uses the electricity generation from solid 
fossil fuels in Poland as the total generation by 
mistake. Option B uses the figure for electricity 
generation by solid fossil fuels in Türkiye instead of 
from renewables and biofuels to determine the target 
percentage. Option E is the target level of electricity 
generation from renewables and biofuels in Poland, 
instead of the amount of the increase.

questions.question: What was the change in the percentage share of electricity not generated from solid fossil fuels, renewables and biofuels in Norway from 2017 to 2021?
questions.explanation:
Reasoning
We must first separately evaluate the percentage 
shares of electricity generation not from solid fossil 
fuels, renewable and biofuels in Norway in 2017 and 
in 2019. We can then calculate the change in these 
percentage shares.
Calculation
The percentage share of electricity generation not 
from solid fossil fuels, renewable and biofuels in 
Norway in 2017:
= 100% – (((43 + 146 225) / 149 156) * 100%)
= 100% – ((146 268 / 149 156) * 100%)
≈ 100% – 98.1%
≈ 1.9%
And the same in 2021:
= 100% – (((44 + 156507) / 157 583) * 100%)
= 100% – ((156 551 / 157 583) * 100%)
≈ 100% – 99.3%
≈ 0.7%
The change in the percentage share of electricity not 
generated from solid fossil fuels, renewables and 
biofuels in Norway from 2017 to 2021:
= 0.7% – 1.9% 
= –1.2%
Option B is the closest in value to our approximate 
solution and the correct choice for an answer.
Potential Shortcuts / Pitfalls
You can find the correct option to choose by thinking 
about the information in the table rather than doing 
lengthy calculations. Norway’s electricity generation 
from solid fossil fuels was clearly negligible in both 
years and therefore can be disregarded. In contrast, 
generation from renewables and biofuels accounted 
for around 98% of total electricity generation in 2017. 
Hence, only around 2% of total generation was not 
from those sources. In 2021, the percentage from 
renewables and biofuels went even higher and was 
close to 99%. Therefore, the percentage share of 
total generation not from renewables and biofuels 
could have only gone down and must be close to 1%. 
Option B is the best choice for your answer.

250
numerical reasoning mixed questions test

In an EPSO abstract reasoning test you are presented with a series of figures in a sequence 
which follows some rules you are not told. You are also given a second set of figures (A, B, 
C, D, E) as answer options, from which you must choose the correct one to answer the ques­
tion “Which of the following figures comes next in the series?” (or similar).  
   It can certainly be a daunting experience to face such a test for the first time. Your eyes 
will tend to scan the shapes of the figures in a haphazard fashion. You finally get an idea 
about the possible rules, and then look at the figures in the answer options to see which 
one fits. And then you realise that the figure you carefully selected would only fit the 
series pattern if just one thing was a little bit different. So now you must start all over 
again... 
   But, just as in the case of verbal and numerical reasoning tests, understanding the prin­
ciples involved and a systematic approach to applying them can produce results much 
more reliably and quickly.  
   As part of such a systematic approach, let us therefore first review the various abstract 
reasoning test types, which are based on: 
•   the use or avoidance of colours 
•   the logical relationship between the various figures, whether they are part of a series, 
a grid, or if there is one figure which is the odd-one-out 
•   the number of dimensions in the test (two- or three-dimensional tests both exist) 
   Fortunately, it is now known that EPSO have chosen a type of abstract reasoning test 
that is very well defined: 
•   only black-and-white images are used (possibly with various shades of grey) 
7.
Succeeding in Abstract Reasoning Tests
